subject: Physics
q: Which branch of physics DOES NOT deal with energy?
skill: Describe the importance of physics in science, technology and society
feedback: All branches of physics deal with energy. Mechanics deals mainly with mechanical energy. Electricity and magnetism deals with electrical energy. Geophysics deals with the energy under the Earth, in its hot core, and how it causes earthquakes and shifting of the continents.

subject: Physics
q: Which branch of physics deals with energy?
skill: Describe the importance of physics in science, technology and society
feedback: All the given branches of physics deal with energy. The branch of sound deals with sound energy. The branch of heat deals with heat energy. The branch of nuclear physics deals with nuclear energy.

subject: Physics
q: Before the eighteenth century, the subjects of biology and physics were known as _____.
skill: Describe the importance of physics in science, technology and society
feedback: Before the 18\[^{th}\] century, biology and physics were studied together as one subject called natural philosophy. As more and more knowledge was gathered natural philosophy was divided into two subjects or sciences: Physical sciences - which deal with the study of non-living things and Biological sciences - which deal with the study of living things.

subject: Physics
q:
What are the main reasons behind the amazing technological advancements humans have made in the last century?

  1. advancements in physics
  2. advancements in biology
  3. human health is better
  4. advancements in mathematics and computation
skill: Describe the importance of physics in science, technology and society
feedback:
The main reason behind the spectacular advances in technology in recent years is the advancement in physics, mathematics and our ability to calculate (computations).

Everything around us is because of advancements in physics. From lightbulbs to fans, from bicycles to cars, from buses to aeroplanes, from mobile phones to televisions, from roads to runways, from X-rays to CT scans. All of these services and facilities are possible due to advancements in physics.

Nowadays, computers control almost everything. Our calculators and mobile phones are also computors. And wherever computers are involved, it points towards progress in mathematics and computation.

subject: Physics
q: Which of the following is a field that relies or depends on physics?
skill: Describe the importance of physics in science, technology and society
feedback:
All of these fields rely or depend on physics in some form or another.

In medical sciences numerous processes and equipment use physics for their operation. For example, microscopes are very important in medical studies. Microscopes are created by physicists and engineers who use the laws of physics in their creation and operation.
Astronomy is the study of the stars. It cannot be done without equipment designed through the use of Physics.
Atomic energy is a field of physics.

subject: Physics
q:
Convert the given number into scientific notation.

\[250 \ \mu\]m
skill: Identify scientific notation in measurements and convert prefixes to scientific notation and vice versa
feedback:
Converting units with prefixes into scientific notation is easy because they already have values in powers of \[10\]. For example, we know that \[\mu\] stands for micro which is equal to \[10^{-6}\]. Therefore, \[250 \ \mu\]m is \[250 \times 10^{-6}\] m.

Now we need to convert this number into scientific notation so that it has only one digit before the decimal. \[250\] is the same as \[250.0\].

So, we need to move the decimal to the left by two digits. To do that, we will add \[2\] to the power of \[10\] which will then become \[10^{-6(+2) = 10^{-4}}\]. The final answer is \[2.5 \times 10^{-4}\].

subject: Physics
q:
Convert the given number into scientific notation.

\[2.5\] GB
skill: Identify scientific notation in measurements and convert prefixes to scientific notation and vice versa
feedback: Converting units with prefixes into scientific notation is easy because they already have values in powers of \[10\]. For example, we know that G stands for giga which is equal to \[10^9\]. Therefore, \[2.5\] GB is simply \[2.5 \times 10^9\] B.

subject: Physics
q: Which of the following numbers is equal to the given number in scientific notation: \[2.54 \times 10^6\]?
skill: Identify scientific notation in measurements and convert prefixes to scientific notation and vice versa
feedback: To convert the given scientific notational number to the regular format, we have to multiply \[2.54\] with \[10^6\]. An easy way to do that is to move the decimal to the right by \[6\] digits. Add \[0\]s to complete the count. For example, we run out of numbers after we move the decimal to the right \[2\] times, first after \[5\] and then after \[4\]. But we need to move it \[6\] digits to the right because the power of \[10\] is \[6\]. Therefore, we need to add four \[0\]s after \[4\] to get the complete number: \[2{,}540{,}000\].

subject: Physics
q: What is \[0.0058\] m in scientific notation?
skill: Identify scientific notation in measurements and convert prefixes to scientific notation and vice versa
feedback:
In scientific notation, the unit or number is represented in a way that it has only one digit before the decimal multiplied by some power of \[10\]. For the given number \[0.0058\], the decimal needs to be moved by \[3\] digits to the right to come after \[5\] so the power of \[10\] will be \[-3\]. This means the resulting scientific notation will be: \[5.8 \times 10^{-3}\].

TRICK: when trying to convert a number into scientific notation we need to calculate what number to put as the power of \[10\]. A trick to do that is to count the number of digits that you have to move the decimal. If we take the example of \[4500.0\], we need to move the decimal \[3\] digits to the left to bring it next to the first digit of the number. Therefore, the power of \[10\] used is \[3\]: \[4.5 \times 10^3\].
Do this trick for the number given in the question.

q: Which one of the following is the smallest quantity?
skill: Identify scientific notation in measurements and convert prefixes to scientific notation and vice versa
feedback:
To compare these quantities, we first convert all these quantities into grams 'g'

\[0.01\] g

\[2\] mg \[= 2 \times 10^{-3}\] g \[= 0.002\] g

\[100\] \[\mu\]g \[= 100 \times 10^{-6}\] g \[= 0.0001\] g

\[5000\] ng \[= 5000 \times 10^{-9}\] g \[= 0.000005\] g

So, \[= 0.000005\] g or \[5000\] ng is the smallest quantity.

q: Which statement is true for volume?
options: Volume is a base quantity and measured in metres.
Volume is a derived quantity and measured in metres.
Volume is a base quantity and measured in cubic metres.
Volume is a derived quantity and measured in cubic metres.
skill: Explain how science is based on physical quantities with examples and differentiate between base and derived physical quantities
feedback: Volume is derived from the base quantity length, which is measured in metres. Volume is the amount of space an object occupies and is obtained by multiplying length into width into height. Therefore, it is measured in cubic metres or m\[^3\].

subject: Physics
q: Select the best description of SI units.
options: SI units are a form of currency.
SI units is a system by which intellect is measured.
SI units are the intelligence agencies that work across the world.
SI units are the internationally standardised units.
skill: Explain how science is based on physical quantities with examples and differentiate between base and derived physical quantities
feedback: SI units are the International System of Units. The SI is abbreviated from the original word in French: Le Système international d'unités. It is the most widely used system of measurement.

subject: Physics
q: A physical quantity consists of _____.
options: a numerical magnitude and a unit
neither a unit nor a numerical magnitude
skill: Explain how science is based on physical quantities with examples and differentiate between base and derived physical quantities
feedback: A physical quantity is anything that can be measured. To be measured, a physical quantity needs to have both a numerical magnitude and a unit to show <u>what</u> is being measured and <u>how much</u> quantity there is.

subject: Physics
q: Which is an accurate example of a physical quantity?
skill: Explain how science is based on physical quantities with examples and differentiate between base and derived physical quantities
feedback: A physical quantity is anything that can be measured. Time can be measured in seconds, minutes, hours, or days and so on. Beauty, truth and love are subjective and cannot be measured exactly.

subject: Physics
q: What is the scientific definition of a physical quantity?
options: A physical quantity is anything that is solid, liquid, or gas.
A physical quantity is anything that can be measured.
A physical quantity is anything that we can see, hear, touch, smell, or taste.
A physical quantity is anything that can be weighed.
skill: Explain how science is based on physical quantities with examples and differentiate between base and derived physical quantities
feedback: A physical quantity is anything that can be measured. Not all quantities can be categorised as solid, liquid or gas, or limited to our sense. For example, time is not limited to our senses, but can be measured.

subject: Physics
q: Which one of the following units is not a derived unit?
skill: Explain how science is based on physical quantities with examples and differentiate between base and derived physical quantities
feedback:
Derived unit is a SI unit of measurement comprised of a combination of the seven base units. Pascal, Newton, and Watt are derived units.

\[1\] N \[= 1\] kgms\[^{-2}\] derived from the formula for Force: F \[=\] ma.
\[1\] Pa \[= 1\] N/\[1\]m\[^2\] \[= 1\] kgms\[^{-2}\]/m\[^2 = 1\] kgm\[^{-1}\]s\[^{-2}\] derived from the formula for pressure: P \[=\] F/A.

Kilogram is not a derived unit.

subject: Physics
q: The number of base units in S.I are _____.
skill: Explain how science is based on physical quantities with examples and differentiate between base and derived physical quantities
feedback:
There are \[7\] base quantities and \[7\] S.I base units:

Mass (kilogram), length (meter), time (second), temperature (kelvin), amount of substance (mole), current (ampere), intensity of light (candela).

subject: Physics
q: The amount of a substance in terms of numbers is measured in _____.
skill: Explain how science is based on physical quantities with examples and differentiate between base and derived physical quantities
feedback: Mole is the S.I unit that is used to measure the amount of substance in terms of numbers.

subject: Physics
q: What is the number of significant digits in \[2,505\]?
skill: Apply the rules for rounding a number to given significant figures to solve problems and discuss the need for significant figures
feedback:
Let us look at all the significant digits in this number.
The non-zero digits are all significant digits.
The zero between the non-zero digits is significant.
Therefore, all the digits in this number are significant digits.

subject: Physics
q: The number of significant digits in the number \[0.003005\] is _____.
skill: Apply the rules for rounding a number to given significant figures to solve problems and discuss the need for significant figures
feedback:
Let us look at all the significant digits in this number.

The zero digits before the decimal and after the decimal before the first non-zero digit ('4' in this case) are not significant. Therefore, the significant digits start with 4.

The non-zero digits are both significant digits.
The zeros between the non-zero digits are significant.
Therefore, the significant figures are: 3, 5, 0, and 0 \[\rightarrow\] 4 digits.

subject: Physics
q: What is the number of significant digits in \[7005.0\]?
skill: Apply the rules for rounding a number to given significant figures to solve problems and discuss the need for significant figures
feedback:
Let us look at all the significant digits in this number.
The non-zero digits are both significant digits.
The zeros between the non-zero digits are significant.
The final zero is also significant because it is the first doubtful number and refers to the precision of the number. Therefore, all the digits in this number are significant.

subject: Physics
q: The significant digits in a number is also influenced by _____.
options: the accuracy and precision of a measuring device
how many zeros come before a number on the left side
how many zeros come on the left side of decimal to space it out
skill: Apply the rules for rounding a number to given significant figures to solve problems and discuss the need for significant figures
feedback:
The significant digits in a number are decided by a few rules and by how important those digits are to convey the numerical information. The significant digits are also influenced by the accuracy and precision of a measuring device.

For instance, a student tries to measure how long it takes for a ball to fall to the ground from a certain height to measure its speed. He reports that it takes 1.00 seconds to reach the ground from the table. But the stopwatch that he was using only tells the time in seconds. Therefore, he could only tell the time accurately down to 1 second. The '.00' falls out of the precision of the stopwatch and so it is not going to be part of the significant figures.

subject: Physics
q: What are the significant figures or digits in the measurement \[0.00450\] kg?
skill: Apply the rules for rounding a number to given significant figures to solve problems and discuss the need for significant figures
feedback:
There are \[3\] significant digits in the measurement \[0.00450\] kg.

The digits \[4\] and \[5\] are non-zero digits (non-zero digits are always significant); the zero after \[5\] is an ending zero (ending or final zeros on the right side in decimal fractions are always significant), while the first \[3\] zeros are not significant as they are used to add space after the decimal point.

subject: Physics
q: The significant figures in an expression are
options: all the accurately known digits and all the doubtful digits.
all the accurately known digits and the first doubtful digit.
skill: Apply the rules for rounding a number to given significant figures to solve problems and discuss the need for significant figures
feedback: All the accurately known digits and the first doubtful digits in an expression are called significant figures. It reflects the precision of a measured value of a physical quantity.

subject: Physics
q: In which case can we speak of the physical quantity of something?
options: when we can put an exact number on the size of an object by measuring it
when we can tell something is very beautiful
when we talk about an object in general
when we can perform chemical experiments with an object
skill: Identify and describe the working of measuring instruments
feedback: The physical quantities of an object are the characteristics of an object that we can measure with a measuring instrument. For example, the length and mass of an object are physical quantities.

subject: Physics
q: When you measure the volume of an object with a measuring cylinder, where should you take the measurement?
options: at the bottom or top of the meniscus
at the place on the cylinder where the liquid curves up or down
at the highest spot of the liquid
at the lowest spot of the liquid
skill: Identify and describe the working of measuring instruments
feedback:
The meniscus is the flat middle part of the top of the liquid in a measuring cylinder. The sides of the liquid may curve upwards or downwards because of the attraction of the liquid to the cylinder. When the cylinder is filled with water, the sides curve upwards.
We need to use the flat middle part, called the bottom of the meniscus, to take the measurement.

subject: Physics
q:
There are different measurement instruments that can measure length. Some measurement instruments are suitable for larger scales, and some are more suitable for smaller scales.

Order the following measurement instruments which are suitable from smallest to largest.
options: \[1.\] Measuring tape; \[2.\] Vernier calliper; \[3.\] Screw gauge; \[4.\] Metre rule
\[1.\] Vernier calliper; \[2.\] Screw gauge; \[3.\] Metre rule; \[4.\] Measuring tape
\[1.\] Screw gauge; \[2.\] Metre rule; \[3.\] Measuring tape; \[4.\] Vernier calliper
\[1.\] Screw gauge; \[2.\] Vernier calliper; \[3.\] Metre rule; \[4.\] Measuring tape
skill: Identify and describe the working of measuring instruments
feedback:
Some measuring instruments are meant to measure metres, some are better suited for measuring centimetres, while some are designed to measure millimetres.

A screw gauge is an instrument that can measure distances of less than a millimetre. For example, it can be used to measure the thickness of a wire.
A Vernier calliper can be used to measure length in millimetres, up to 10 centimetres. For example, it can be used to measure the diameter of a tube.
A metre rule is used to measure length in millimetres up to a metre. For example, it can measure the length of a book.
A measuring tape can measure the longest distance, usually several metres. For example, you use a measuring tape to measure the length of a room.

subject: Physics
q: What is a meniscus?
options: the place where a liquid touches the cylinder in a measuring cylinder
the liquid in a measuring cylinder, which we can use to measure volume
meniscus is another word for a measuring cylinder
the meniscus is the surface of the liquid in a measuring cylinder
skill: Identify and describe the working of measuring instruments
feedback: the meniscus is the upper surface of the liquid in a measuring cylinder. When we take the measurement in a measuring cylinder, we look at the bottom or top of the meniscus. We don't take the measurement at the place where the liquid touches the cylinder.

subject: Physics
q: Which of the following is NOT a physical quantity?
skill: Identify and describe the working of measuring instruments
feedback: A physical quantity is a quantity that we can measure. Two different people measuring the same body, will measure the same number. Mass (kg), length (m) and time (s) are all physical quantities. Love is not a physical quantity, since we cannot objectively measure it.

subject: Physics
q: You want to buy a new table cloth for the dining table. Which instrument would you use to measure the length of the table?
skill: Identify and describe the working of measuring instruments
feedback: A measuring tape would be most suitable to measure the size of a table. A measuring tape can measure anything between a centimetre and a few metres. A screw gauge and a Vernier calliper are designed to measure smaller distances, while a measuring cylinder is designed to measure volume.

subject: Physics
q:
You're a plumber and looking for a metal tube for a job in a shop. You know the exact size of the hole that the tube should go through: it's 2.62 cm.

Which would be the best measuring instrument to use to help you select the right one?
skill: Identify and describe the working of measuring instruments
feedback: For sizes between 1 mm and 10 cm, a Vernier calliper is the best instrument. A Vernier calliper has the advantage that it can measure both the inside and the outside of the tube.

subject: Physics
q:
Sumaira is interested to know the volume of a coin. She takes the following steps:

I. She completely submerges the coin in the liquid in a measuring cylinder
II. She is careful to read the liquid column at the meniscus
III. She sees that the measuring cylinder reads 5.8 cm3.
IV. She concludes that the coin has a volume of 5.8 cm3.

Where did Sumaira make an error in the measurement?
options: She should have read the liquid column at the edge of the cylinder.
She should not have completely submerged the coin in the liquid.
She should have also taken a measurement before she submerged the coin.
She should not have measured the coin in cubic centimetres (cm<sup>3</sup>).
skill: Identify and describe the working of measuring instruments
feedback: Sumaira has read the liquid column at the correct place, and she was right to completely submerge the coin. However, she did not read the cylinder before submerging the coin, so she can't calculate the difference in volume (V = V<sub>2</sub> - V<sub>1</sub>). Finally, she can measure the coin in cubic centimetres, but she may end up with decimals.

subject: Physics
q:
We have a measuring cylinder, two pebbles, and we are making a number of measurements.

First, we read the meniscus of the measuring cylinder when it only has water. The cylinder indicates 5.6 cm3. Then we take two different measurements.

We submerge two pebbles of different sizes in the cylinder. The cylinder reads 11.3 cm3. Then, we remove the smaller pebble in the cylinder. Now the cylinder reads 9.8 cm3.

What is the volume of the larger pebble?
skill: Identify and describe the working of measuring instruments
feedback:
The volume of the liquid without any pebbles is 5.6 cm3. After putting in both pebbles, we measure 11.3 cm3. We can now calculate the volume of both pebbles with V = V2-V1, which is 11.3 - 5.6 = 5.7 cm3.
Then we measure the first, smallest pebble. We measure 11.3 - 9.8 = 1.5 cm3.
Now we can conclude that the other pebble has a volume of 5.7 - 1.5 = 4.2 cm3.

subject: Physics
q:
Mahnum has her eyes level with the meniscus in a measuring cylinder with water, and reads the bottom of the meniscus. She reads 8.7 cm3. Ahmed stands next to her and looks down at the measuring cylinder, and reads 8.9 cm3.

Who made a mistake, and what should they have done better?
options: Mahnum made a mistake. She should have read the edge of the meniscus.
Mahnum made a mistake. She should have stood up to read the meniscus.
Ahmed made a mistake. He should have read the edge of the meniscus.
Ahmed made a mistake. He should have had his eyes level with the meniscus.
skill: Identify and describe the working of measuring instruments
feedback:
When we read a measuring cylinder, we should always read the bottom of the meniscus, and we should keep our eyes level with the meniscus.

Ahmed was looking down at the measuring cylinder. If he looks down, he can't get a good reading of the meniscus.

subject: Physics
q:
Faraz measures the length of his textbook with a metre rule, and measures 26.5 cm. Then Ayesha measures the same textbook with her metre rule and measures 27.5 cm.

What will most likely have gone wrong?
options: The two metre rules use different scales.
Metre rules are not very reliable, and different metre rules give different readings.
The book has increased in size between the two measures.
One of them has not started the measurement at the zero of the metre rule.
skill: Identify and describe the working of measuring instruments
feedback: A metre rule often has some space before the zero, so that it can still be useful even if it has some damage. It is most likely that one of the students has not started the measurements at the zero of the metre rule.

subject: Physics
q:
Three students are each given a pebble, and each student measures their pebble in a measuring cylinder. The three students measure \[2.3\] cm\[^3\], \[250\] mm\[^3\], and \[0.021\] dm\[^3\].
Can you order the pebbles from smallest to largest?
skill: Identify and describe the working of measuring instruments
feedback:
The three pebbles have been measured in three different units. We first calculate the volume of each pebble in cm3.
250 mm3 = 0.23 cm3
0.021 dm3 = 21 cm3
Now we can quickly see that the pebble measured in mm3 is the smallest, and the pebble measured in dm3 is the largest.
The pebble of 21 cm3 is actually so big, that it doesn't qualify as a pebble anymore. It's likely that the student measuring that pebble has made an error.

subject: Physics
q:
Abdullah found a perfectly round marble, and he wants to measure the volume of his marble. Which of the following methods can Abdullah use to measure the volume of the marble?

I. Abdullah can put the marble in a measuring cylinder. He can take the measurement at the meniscus before and after he puts in the marble, and then calculate the difference.
II. Abdullah can put the marble on a precise scale. The scale will tell him the volume of the marble.
III. Abdullah can use a Vernier calliper and measure the diameter of the marble. With the diameter of the marble, he can calculate the volume.
skill: Identify and describe the working of measuring instruments
feedback:
A measuring cylinder is a measurement tool designed to measure the volume of an object. A scale is created for measuring mass by weighing objects.

A Vernier calliper can be used to measure the diameter of an object. We know, however, that the marble is round, so we can measure the volume of the marble with the formula V=(4⁄3)π3, where r is the radius of the marble.

subject: Physics
q:
Wadood wants to measure the volume of a piece of cork. He takes a measuring cylinder and fills it halfway with water, and reads the meniscus at the bottom of the meniscus. Then he drops the cork on the water, and he reads the meniscus again. He now subtracts the first reading from the last reading.

What did Wadood do wrong?
options: Wadood should have read the edge of the meniscus.
Corks float. Wadood should have made the cork submerge in the water.
Wadood should use the reading on the measuring cylinder to know the volume of the cork.
Wadood should have subtracted the last reading from the first reading.
skill: Identify and describe the working of measuring instruments
feedback: Corks float, since they are lighter than water. When an object floats, only part of it will be under water. The rise of the water in the measuring cylinder will not be the equivalent of the volume of the cork, if the cork does not submerge completely.

subject: Physics
q: The least count of a screw gauge is _____.
skill: Identify and describe the working of measuring instruments
feedback:
The least count of screw gauge can be found as given below:

\[ \mbox{ Least count } = \frac{\mbox{pitch of the screw gauge}}{\mbox{no. of divisions on circular scale}} \]

\[ \mbox{ Least count } = \frac{1 \mbox{mm}}{100} = 0.01\] mm or \[ 0.001\] cm

subject: Physics
q: For an object to be in vibratory motion, its _____ needs to remain the same.
skill: Define rest and motion and describe different types of motion
feedback:
In vibratory motion, the object vibrates about the same mean position. Its mean position does not change.

The velocity and acceleration of the object keeps changes as it vibrates about one point. This will be studied later when you study waves and vibrations.

subject: Physics
q: During a game of cricket, a batsman hits a ball for four runs down the ground. The ball undergoes _____ motion. Assume that the ball does not spin as it goes down the ground.
skill: Define rest and motion and describe different types of motion
feedback: Linear motion is motion in a straight path. As the ball travels straight down the ground, its motion is straight. This means the motion of the ball is linear motion.

subject: Physics
q: If a body does not change its position _____, it is said to be at rest.
skill: Define rest and motion and describe different types of motion
feedback: If a body does not change its position with respect to its surroundings, it is said to be at rest. An example of this is your notebook as it lays on the table. It is not moving so we know that it is at rest. But we know that the Earth is consistently moving which means that the notebook, and the table that it lays on, is also moving. BUT because the notebook is not changing its position with respect to its surroundings, it is considered to be at rest.

subject: Physics
q: The motion of a body about an axis is called
skill: Define rest and motion and describe different types of motion
feedback: The spinning motion of a body about its axis is called rotatory motion. For example, a wheel is in rotatory motion when it moves about its axis.

subject: Physics
q: A body has a translatory motion if it moves along a
skill: Define rest and motion and describe different types of motion
feedback:
In a translational motion, a body moves along a line without any rotation. The line may be straight or curved. Translatory motion can be divided into three types of motion: linear, circular, and random.

* The straight line motion of a body is known as linear motion.

* The motion of an object in a circular path is known as circular motion.

* The irregular motion of an object is called random motion.

subject: Physics
q:
The rocket is flying straight up in the air at \[350\] ms\[^{-1}\].

What information about the rocket does the above statement provide?
skill: Differentiate between distance and displacement, speed and velocity
feedback: The given information tells us the speed of the rocket (\[350\] ms\[^{-1}\]) including its direction (straight up). This means that this piece of information is the velocity of the rocket.

subject: Physics
q:
\[350\] kilometres to the north-east.

The given information is the _____ of an object.
skill: Differentiate between distance and displacement, speed and velocity
feedback: The information that we have been given here has both distance AND direction. This means that it is the displacement of an object.

subject: Physics
q: Distance and speed are _____ quantities, and displacement and velocity are _____ quantities.
skill: Differentiate between distance and displacement, speed and velocity
feedback:
Distance and speed are scalar quantities. They only have magnitude and no direction.

Displacement and velocity are vector quantities. The have both magnitude and direction.

subject: Physics
q: Which of the following statements about displacement is INCORRECT?
options: It is the shortest distance from point A to point B.
skill: Differentiate between distance and displacement, speed and velocity
feedback:
Displacement is the shortest distance between two points. For example, if you walk in a straight line from point \[O\] to point \[B\] and then turn back to point \[A\], your distance-travelled will be \[15\] m (\[10\] m to point \[B\] and then \[5\] m back to point \[A\]). But your displacement will only be \[5\] m as \[5\] m is the shortest distance from \[O\] to \[A\].



Displacement can also be negative. If you had continued walking back towards \[O\] and then kept moving in the opposite direction, your displacement would become negative.

subject: Physics
q: A change in position is called _____.
skill: Differentiate between distance and displacement, speed and velocity
feedback:
A change in the position of a body is called displacement.

It is the shortest distance between the object and its original position, regardless of the distance travelled. For example, if you travel \[5\] metres forward and then \[4\] metres backwards, you will have covered a total distance of \[5+4=9\] m, but your displacement from your original location will only be \[5-4=1\] m.

The distance covered by an object in unit time is called its speed.

The rate of displacement of a body is called velocity.

subject: Physics
q: A change in the position of a body is called _____.
skill: Differentiate between distance and displacement, speed and velocity
feedback:
A change in the position of a body is called displacement.

The distance covered by an object, regardless of its final position, is called distance.

The distance covered by an object in unit time is called its speed.

The rate of displacement of a body is called velocity.

subject: Physics
q: Which of the following is a vector?
skill: Define scalar and vector quantities, and differentiate between them using examples
feedback: The first indication that \[\overrightarrow{F}\] is the vector is the arrow above the \[F\]. For further clues, we can see that \[S\] is distance which is a scalar quantity, \[m\] is mass which is also a scalar, and \[P\] is pressure also a scalar. \[F\] is force which is a vector \[\overrightarrow{F}\].

subject: Physics
q: Which statement is true about the following diagram? Both \[\overrightarrow{v_1}\] and \[\overrightarrow{v_2}\] start at the origin of the graph.

subject: Physics
q: Which of the following is not a vector quantity?
skill: Define scalar and vector quantities, and differentiate between them using examples
feedback:
A vector quantity is described completely by its magnitude and direction. Pressure only has magnitude so pressure is not a vector quantity.

Momentum, weight, and torque are vector quantities.

subject: Physics
q: Which of the following is NOT a vector quantity?
skill: Define scalar and vector quantities, and differentiate between them using examples
feedback:
A vector quantity has both magnitude and direction. Speed only has a magnitude and no direction so speed is not a vector quantity.

Force, velocity, and momentum are vector quantities.

subject: Physics
q: A car travelling at \[80\] kmh\[^{-1}\] decelerates with an acceleration of \[-20\] kmh\[^{-2}\] until it comes to rest in \[4\] seconds. What is its final velocity?
skill: Define the terms speed, velocity and acceleration
feedback: Whenever any object comes to a stop or comes to rest its velocity is \[0\]. We can find the final velocity by using the formula for acceleration, but we do not need to. The question states that the car comes to rest, therefore its final velocity is \[0\] kmh\[^{-1}\].

subject: Physics
q: If a body is travelling at a high uniform velocity, its acceleration _____.
skill: Define the terms speed, velocity and acceleration
feedback: Acceleration is the rate of change of velocity. If the velocity is constant, does not matter if it is high or low, the acceleration will always be zero. Because if the acceleration is NOT zero then it means the velocity is changing is therefore, not constant.

subject: Physics
q: If a body has an acceleration of \[0\] ms\[^{-2}\] or \[0\] kmh\[^{-2}\], its velocity _____.
skill: Define the terms speed, velocity and acceleration
feedback: Acceleration is the rate of change of velocity. If the acceleration of a body is \[0\], it means its velocity is not changing. For example, if an aeroplane is travelling at a velocity of \[250\] ms\[^{-1}\] and has an acceleration of \[0\] ms\[^{-2}\], then its velocity will remain \[250\] ms\[^{-1}\] until the pilot increases the acceleration from \[0\] ms\[^{-2}\] (it will make the aeroplane fly faster). The pilot can also decrease the acceleration from \[0\] ms\[^{-2}\] into a negative value if he or she wants to slow the plane down.

subject: Physics
q: When a body is undergoing uniform acceleration its velocity _____.
skill: Define the terms speed, velocity and acceleration
feedback: When a body is travelling with uniform or constant acceleration, its velocity increases at a constant rate. For example, if a car was travelling with a constant acceleration of \[5\] ms\[^{-2}\], it's velocity will increase at a constant rate of \[5\] ms\[^{-2}\] every second. If at one second it was \[15\] ms\[^{-1}\], the next second it will be \[20\] ms\[^{-1}\] and the second after that it will be \[25\] ms\[^{-1}\] and so on.

subject: Physics
q: If a rocket takes off with an initial velocity of \[50\] ms\[^{-1}\] and then accelerates further at a constant acceleration of \[85\] ms\[^{-2}\]. What will be its velocity after \[10\] seconds?
skill: Define the terms speed, velocity and acceleration
feedback:
: To calculate the velocity of the rocket after \[10\] seconds, simply input the known values into the acceleration formula. The known values are:

\[a = 85\] ms\[^{-2}\]
\[v_i = 50\] ms\[^{-1}\]
\[v_f = ?\] ms\[^{-1}\]
\[t = 10 s\]

\begin{align} a &= \frac{v_f-v_i}{t} \\ \\ 85 &= \frac{v_f - 50}{10} \\ \\ 85 \times 10 &= v_f-50 \\ v_f &= 850 + 50 \\ &= 900 \mbox{ ms}^{-1} \end{align}

subject: Physics
q: A car travelling at \[60\] ms\[^{-1}\] starts braking with a deceleration of \[30\] ms\[^{-2}\]. How long will it take before it comes to a stop?
skill: Define the terms speed, velocity and acceleration
feedback:
To calculate the deceleration of the car, simply input the known values into the acceleration formula. But remember, deceleration is negative acceleration.

\[a = -30\] ms\[^{-2}\]
\[v_i = 60\] ms\[^{-1}\]
\[v_f = 0\] ms\[^{-1}\], because the car comes to a stop.
\[t = ?\]

\begin{align} a &= \frac{v_f-v_i}{t} \\ \\ -30 &= \frac{0 - 60}{t} \\ \\ t &= \frac{-60}{-30} &= 2 \mbox{ s} \end{align}

subject: Physics
q: Which one of the following statements best describes a body that has an acceleration of \[20\] ms\[^{-2}\]?
options: The body will travel at a constant velocity of \[20\] ms\[^{-1}\].
The body will come to a stop after reaching \[20\] ms\[^{-1}\].
The body will always have a velocity than \[20\] ms\[^{-1}\].
The body will be \[20\] ms\[^{-1}\] faster after every \[1\] second.
skill: Define the terms speed, velocity and acceleration
feedback: Acceleration stands for the change in velocity per unit time. It tells us how quickly or slowly a body is changing its velocity. An acceleration of \[20\] ms\[^{-2}\] means that the body's velocity will be \[20\] ms\[^{-1}\] higher each second. If at the 5th second its velocity is \[60\] ms\[^{-1}\], at the 6th second its velocity will be \[80\] ms\[^{-1}\].

subject: Physics
q: A motorbike travelling at \[20\] kmh\[^{-1}\] increases its velocity up to \[60\] kmh\[^{-1}\] in \[144\] seconds. What is the bike's acceleration in kmh\[^{-2}\]?
skill: Define the terms speed, velocity and acceleration
feedback:
To calculate the bike's acceleration in kmh\[^{-2}\], first convert the given time (\[144\] seconds) into hours and then apply the formula for acceleration.

\[144 s = \frac{144}{3600} hrs = 0.04 hrs\]
\[v_i = 20\] kmh\[^{-1}\]
\[v_f = 60\] kmh\[^{-1}\]
\[t = 0.04 hrs\] \begin{align} a &= \frac{v_f-v_i}{t} \\ \\ &= \frac{60 - 20}{0.04} \\ \\ &= 1000 \mbox{ kmh}^{-2} \end{align}

subject: Physics
q: What of the following formulas represents deceleration or retardation?
skill: Define the terms speed, velocity and acceleration
feedback:
Acceleration is the change in velocity per unit time. When the change in velocity is positive, then it is simply called acceleration. But when the change in velocity is negative, it is called deceleration or retardation. Therefore, deceleration has the same formula as acceleration:

\[a = \frac{v_f-v_i}{t}\]

When a car travelling at 30 kmh\[^{-1}\] increases its speed up to 100 kmh\[^{-1}\], it accelerates.
When a car travelling at 120 kmh\[^{-1}\] slows down to a speed of \[80\] kmh\[^{-1}\] it decelerates.

subject: Physics
q: What is the formula for acceleration?
skill: Define the terms speed, velocity and acceleration
feedback:
Acceleration is the change in velocity per unit time. Change in velocity is the difference between the initial velocity (\[v_i\]) and the final velocity (v_f). This difference between velocities is then divided by time to know how quickly or slowly the velocity increases or decreases. The formula for acceleration is:

\[a = \frac{v_f-v_i}{t}\]

subject: Physics
q: The car below covers the displacement from point \[A\] to \[B\] in \[30\] minutes. Calculate the velocity of the car in ms-1.
skill: Define the terms speed, velocity and acceleration
feedback:
To calculate the velocity in ms-1, first convert the units.

\[1\] km \[=1,000\] m
\[1\] mins \[=60\] s

Distance \[=25\] km \[=25\times 1,000=25,000\] m

Time \[=30\] mins \[=30\times 60=1,800\] s

\[\mbox{Speed} = \frac{\mbox{Distance}}{\mbox{Time}}\]\[=\frac{25,000}{1,800}=13.89\] ms-1

Therefore, the car travelled at a velocity of \[13.89\] ms-1.

A paratrooper attains uniform velocity called terminal velocity while descending or falling to the ground. This occurs when the force of air resistance equals the weight of the falling paratrooper.

A body has uniform velocity if it covers equal displacement in equal intervals of time.

q: By dividing the displacement of a moving body with time, we obtain _____.

By dividing displacement of a moving body with time, we obtain velocity. As velocity is the rate of displacement of a body, given as :

\[ \mbox{Velocity} = \frac{\mbox{displacement}}{\mbox{time taken}} \]

We can find out the speed by dividing distance with time.

subject: Physics
q: A car starts from rest. It acquires a speed of \[25\] ms\[^{-1}\] after \[20\] s. During this time, the car covers a distance of _____.
skill: Define the terms speed, velocity and acceleration
feedback:
As the car starts from rest and reaches a speed of \[25\] ms\[^{-1}\] after \[20\] s, we cannot say that the \[\mbox{distance covered} = \mbox{speed} \times \mbox{time}\] because for this, the speed needs to be the same for all the given time.

To find the solution to this question, we will need to find the area under the graph of the speed-time graph of the car. As the car starts from rest and reaches \[25\] ms\[^{-1}\] after \[20\] s, the graph will look like a triangle with base \[= 20\] s and height \[= 25\] ms\[^{-1}\]:

\begin{align} \mbox{distance covered} = \mbox{area of triangle} &= \frac{1}{2} \times base \times height \\ &= \frac{1}{2} (20)(25) \\ &= 250 \mbox{ m} \\ \end{align}

subject: Physics
q: Find the retardation produced when a car moving at a velocity of \[30\] ms\[^{-1}\] slows down uniformly to \[15\] ms\[^{-1}\] in \[5\] s.

Initial velocity \[v_i = 30\] ms\[^{-1}\]

Final velocity \[v_f = 15\] ms\[^{-1}\]

Time \[ t = 5\] s

Retardation / Deceleration a \[ = ?\]

a \[= \frac {v_f - v_i}{t} = \frac {15 - 30}{5} = \frac {-15}{5} = -3 \] ms\[^{-2}\]

q: A change in position is called _____.
skill: Differentiate between distance and displacement, speed and velocity
feedback:
A change in the position of a body is called displacement.

It is the shortest distance between the object and its original position, regardless of the distance travelled. For example, if you travel \[5\] metres forward and then \[4\] metres backwards, you will have covered a total distance of \[5+4=9\] m, but your displacement from your original location will only be \[5-4=1\] m.

The distance covered by an object in unit time is called its speed.

The rate of displacement of a body is called velocity.

subject: Physics

skill: Differentiate between distance and displacement, speed and velocity
feedback:
A change in the position of a body is called displacement.

The distance covered by an object, regardless of its final position, is called distance.

The distance covered by an object in unit time is called its speed.

The rate of displacement of a body is called velocity.

q: The following graph shows the motion of a car. What is the speed of the car between \[10\] and \[20\] seconds? What is the average velocity of the car for the whole journey (starting from \[0\] seconds)?
skill: Understand distance-time graphs and solve problems
feedback:
As the distance-time graph has a straight line between \[10\] and \[20\] seconds, it means that the car has the same speed throughout that duration. To find that speed, we need to calculate the slope of the graph. Another method is to see that the car was at \[0\] metres at \[10\] seconds and it goes to \[15\] metres at \[20\] seconds. This means, the car covers \[15 - 0 = 15\] metres in \[20 - 10 = 10\] seconds. The speed of the car is:

\begin{align} v &= \frac{d}{t} \\ \\ &= \frac{15}{10} \\ \\ &= \frac{3}{2} = 1.5 \mbox{ m/s} \end{align}

To find the average velocity, we will use the total distance and total time taken. The total distance is \[15\] metres and the total time taken is \[30\] seconds:

\begin{align} v &= \frac{d}{t} \\ \\ &= \frac{15}{30} \\ \\ &= \frac{1}{2} = 0.5 \mbox{ m/s} \end{align}

q: Which of the following options best describes the motion of the body shown in this distance-time graph?
options: The body is moving at an increasing speed.
The body is moving at a constant speed.
The body is moving at a decreasing speed.
skill: Understand distance-time graphs and solve problems
feedback:
The given distance-time graph shows a downward curve. This means that the body is moving with a decreasing speed as it covers a shorter distance in the same intervals of time. In the beginning, the body starts at a high speed (the slope is steepest in the beginning), and then it decreases and flattens, which means the speed is decreasing.

Remember: in a distance-time graph, if a line is straight (which means its slope is constant), the velocity of the body is constant. If the line curves upwards, that means the velocity is increasing. If the line curves downwards, it means the velocity is decreasing.

q: Given the following speed-time graph of a body, calculate its acceleration in the final \[10\] seconds.
skill: Understand speed-time graphs and solve problems
feedback:
To calculate the acceleration of a body using its speed-time graph, we need to calculate the slope of the graph. The question asks for the acceleration in the last \[10\] seconds. We can see that the body starts at velocity \[10\] m/s at \[30\] seconds and goes to a velocity of \[0\] m/s at \[40\] seconds.

\begin{align} \mbox{Acceleration} &= \mbox{Slope of the line from 30 to 40 seconds} \\ \mbox{Acceleration} &= \frac{0-10}{40-30} \\ \\ &= \frac{-10}{10} \\ \\ &= -1 \mbox{ m/s}^2 \end{align}

We can see that the acceleration is negative which means that the car was decelerating. This makes sense because it was going at a speed of \[10\] m/s and then came to a stop at a speed of \[0\] m/s.

subject: Physics
q: If a car is travelling at a speed of \[60\] kmh\[^{-1}\], and the driver wants to increase the speed to \[100\] kmh\[^{-1}\] in \[40\] seconds, what constant acceleration should he have?
skill: Solve word problems related to uniformly accelerated motion using appropriate equations
feedback:
Let us first review all the given information:

Initial velocity: \[v_i = 60\] kmh\[^{-1}\]
Final velocity: \[v_f = 100\] kmh\[^{-1}\]
Time taken: \[t = 40\] seconds
We need to calculate the acceleration, which is \[a \] kmh\[^{-2}\]. However, first, we need to convert the given time from seconds to hours as all the speeds are in kmh\[^{-1}\].

\begin{align} 1 \mbox{ hour} &= 60 \times 60 \mbox{ seconds} \\ 1 \mbox{ hr} &= 3600 \mbox{ s} \\ \frac{1}{3600} \mbox{ hr} &= 1 \mbox{ s} \\ \\ 40 \mbox{ s} &= \frac{1}{3600} \times 40 \mbox{ hr} \\ \\ 40 \mbox{ s} &= 0.0111 \mbox{ hr} \end{align}

For this question, we will use the first equation of motion, as it has all the variables given in the question.

\begin{align} v_f &= v_i + at \\ 100 &= 60 + (a)(0.0111) \\ 100 - 60 &= 0.0111a \\ a &= \frac{40}{0.0111} \\ \\ &= 3600 \mbox{ kmh}^{-2} \end{align}

subject: Physics
q:
Which equation of motion will be used to solve a question that provides the following?
Initial velocity: \[v_i\] ms\[^{-1}\]
Distance: \[S\] m
Final velocity: \[v_f\] ms\[^{-1}\]
Variable acceleration: \[a\] ms\[^{-2}\]
options: first equation of motion: \[v_f = v_i + at\]
third equation of motion: \[2aS = v_f^2 - v_i^2\]
skill: Solve word problems related to uniformly accelerated motion using appropriate equations
feedback: These equations of motion are only applicable when the acceleration is constant. If the acceleration changes, or is variable, then we cannot apply these equations.

subject: Physics
q: Given the figure below what deceleration will the car need to have to stop before colliding with the cart?
skill: Solve word problems related to uniformly accelerated motion using appropriate equations
feedback:
Let us first review all the given information:

Initial velocity: \[v_i = 20\] ms\[^{-1}\]
Distance: \[S = 500\] m
Final velocity: \[v_f = 0\] ms\[^{-1}\]
We need to calculate the acceleration, which is \[a\] ms\[^{-2}\].

For this question, we will use the third equation of motion as it has all the variables provided in the question and the one that we want to calculate: \[a\].
\begin{align} 2aS &= v_f^2 - v_i^2 \\ 2(a)(500) &= (0)^2 - (20)^2 \\ 1000a &= 0 - 400 \\ S &= \frac{-400}{1000} \\ &= -0.4 \mbox{ ms}^{-2} \end{align}

Note: The acceleration is negative: \[-0.4\] ms\[^{-2}\], but when it is called deceleration, it is positive: \[0.4\] ms\[^{-2}\]. The question asks for deceleration, not acceleration.

subject: Physics
q: A car travelling at \[20\] ms\[^{-1}\] gets on the motorway and accelerates at a rate of \[5\] ms\[^{-2}\] for \[10\] s. How much distance does it travel in this time?
skill: Solve word problems related to uniformly accelerated motion using appropriate equations
feedback:
Let us first have a look at all the given information:

Initial velocity: \[v_i = 20\] ms\[^{-1}\]
Acceleration: \[a = 5\] ms\[^{-2}\]
Time taken: \[t = 10\] s
We need to calculate the distance covered in this time, \[S\] m.

For this question, we will use the second equation of motion as it has all the variables given in the question. The second equation of motion contains distance \[S\] covered but not the final velocity \[v_f\].
\begin{align} S &= v_it + \frac{1}{2}at^2 \\ \\ &= (20)(10) + \frac{1}{2}(5)(10)^2 \\ \\ &= 200 + \frac{5}{2}\times 100 \\ \\ &= 200 + 250 \\ &= 450 \mbox{ m} \end{align}

subject: Physics
q: If a moving car undergoes a constant deceleration of \[10\] ms\[^{-2}\] and comes to a stop after \[30\] seconds, what was its initial speed?
skill: Solve word problems related to uniformly accelerated motion using appropriate equations
feedback:
Let us first review all the given information:

Acceleration: \[a = -10\] ms\[^{-2}\] (because the car is decelerating)
Final velocity: \[v_f = 0\] ms\[^{-1}\]
Time: \[t = 30\] s
We need to calculate the initial velocity, \[v_i\] ms\[^{-1}\],

For this question, we will use the first equation of motion as it has all the variables given in the question.
\begin{align} v_f &= v_i + at \\ 0 &= v_i + (-10)(30) \\ 0 &= v_i -300 \\ v_i &= 300 \mbox{ ms}^{-1} \end{align}

subject: Physics
q: A train travelling at \[50\] kmh\[^{-1}\] starts decelerating as it approaches the train station. If the rate of deceleration of the train is \[500\] kmh\[^{-2}\], how long will it take before it comes to a complete stop?
skill: Solve word problems related to uniformly accelerated motion using appropriate equations
feedback:
Let us first review the given information:

Initial velocity: \[v_i = 50\] kmh\[^{-1}\]
Acceleration: \[a = -500\] kmh\[^{-2}\]
Final velocity: \[v_f = 0\] kmh\[^{-1}\]
We need to calculate the time taken \[t\] hrs.

For this question, we will use the first equation of motion as it has all the variables given in the question.
\begin{align} v_f &= v_i + at \\ 0 &= 50 + (-500)(t) \\ 0 &= 50 -500t \\ 500t &= 50 \\ t &= \frac{50}{500} = 0.1 \mbox{ hrs} \end{align}

The next step is to convert the time from hours into seconds:
\[1\] hr \[ = 60 \times 60\] seconds
\[0.1\] hr \[ = 60 \times 60 \times 0.1 = 360\] seconds

subject: Physics
q: A bicycle travelling at a speed of \[3\] ms\[^{-1}\] starts going down a hill. This makes it accelerate uniformly at \[2\] ms\[^{-2}\]. What will its speed be after \[10\] seconds?
skill: Solve word problems related to uniformly accelerated motion using appropriate equations
feedback:
In this question we are given:

Initial velocity: \[v_i = 3\] ms\[^{-1}\]
Acceleration: \[a = 2\] ms\[^{-2}\]
Time: \[t = 10\] s
We need to calculate the final velocity \[v_f\] ms\[^{-1}\].

The first equation of motion will be used here as it contains all the above variables.
\begin{align} v_f &= v_i + at \\ &= 3 + (2)(10) \\ &= 3 + 20 \\ &= 23 \mbox{ ms}^{-1} \end{align}

subject: Physics
q: When brakes are applied, the speed of a train decreases from \[96\] kmh\[^{-1}\] to \[48\] kmh\[^{-1}\] in \[800\] m. How much further will the train move before coming to rest\[?\] Assume the deceleration is constant. Find the time taken by the train to stop after the application of brakes.
skill: Solve word problems related to uniformly accelerated motion using appropriate equations
feedback:
Initial velocity \[ v_i = 96\] kmh\[^{-1} = 96 \times {\frac{1000}{3600}} = 26.67 \] ms\[^{-1}\]

final velocity \[ v_f = 48\] kmh\[^{-1} = 48 \times {\frac{1000}{3600}} = 13.33\] ms\[^{-1}\]

Distance \[ S = 800\] m

The train will cover 'S' distance before coming to rest after applying brakes.

Using the \[3\]rd equation of motion \[2aS = v_f^2 - v_i^2\]

Putting in the values, we get:

\[2a\](\[800\]) \[ = 13.33^2 - 26.67^2\]

or \[ a = - \frac{533.6}{1600} = - 0.33\] ms\[^{-2}\]

When the train comes to rest:

\[v_i = 13.33\] ms\[^{-1}\]

\[v_f = 0\] ms\[^{-1}\]

We will put in these new values in the same equation to now find S: \[2\]aS \[ = v_f^2 - v_i^2\]

putting values in, we get:

\[2\] (\[-0.33\]) \[S = 0^2 - 13.33^2\]

\[-0.66S = -13.33^2\]

\[S = \frac{13.33^2}{0.66} = 266\] m

Therefore, the train will move '\[266\] m' before coming to rest after applying the brakes.

Now, we have to find the time taken by the train to come to a stop after the brakes were applied.

v\[_f = 0\] ms\[^{-1}\]

v\[_i = 26.67\] ms\[^{-1}\]

a \[ = - 0.33\] ms\[^{-2}\]

Using the first equation of motion: v\[_f = \] v\[_i +\] at

Putting the values in, we get:

\[ 0 = 26.67 + (-0.33)\] t

or t \[= \frac{26.67}{0.33} = 80\] s

Hence, the required time will be \[80\] s.

subject: Physics
q: The speed-time graph of a car is shown in the figure below. Which of the following statements is true?
options: The car has an acceleration of \[1.5\] ms\[^{-2}\].
