feedback:
From the given graph, we note:

Speed of car \[=\] v \[= 15 ms^{-1}\]

Time \[=\] t \[= 10\]s

As acceleration \[ = \frac{v_2-v_1}{t} = \frac {0-15}{10 \mbox{s}} = -1.5 \] ms\[^{-2}\]

So, we can say that the car has an acceleration of \[-1.5\] ms\[^{-2}\] and not \[1.5\] ms\[^{-2}\].

Distance travelled by the car is the area under the speed-time graph:

Area under the speed-time graph \[=\] Area of Triangle \[= \frac{1}{2} \times base \times height = \frac{1}{2} (10)(15) = 75\] m.

subject: Physics
q: A train starts from rest with an acceleration of \[0.5\] ms\[^{-2}\]. Find its speed in kmh\[^{-1}\], when it has moved through \[100\] m.
skill: Solve word problems related to uniformly accelerated motion using appropriate equations
feedback:
Initial velocity \[ v_i = 0\] ms\[^{-1}\]

Acceleration \[ a = 0.5\] ms\[^{-2}\]

Distance \[ S = 100\] m

Final velocity \[ v_f = ? \]

Using the third equation of motion:

\[2 a S = v_f^2 - v_i^2\]

\[ 2 \times 0.5 \times 100 \] \[ = v_f^2 - 0 \]

\[v_f^2 = 100 \] ms\[^{-1}\]

\[v_f = 10\] ms\[^{-1}\]

Now we can convert the speed from ms\[^{-1}\] to kmh\[^{-1}\]

\[v_f = 10 \] ms\[^{-1} = \frac{10 \times 3600}{1000} = 36 \] kmh\[^{-1}\]

subject: Physics
q: A ball is thrown into the air with a velocity of \[16\] ms\[^{-1}\]. What will be its velocity at the peak of its height?
skill: Solve word problems related to freely falling bodies
feedback: For free-falling bodies, the velocity at the peak is always \[0\] ms\[^{-1}\]. When the body is travelling upwards, it has an upward velocity. At the peak, the upward velocity becomes \[0\], and then the object starts accelerating downwards, starting from \[0\] velocity.

subject: Physics
q: A bullet is fired upwards into the air. It comes back down and hits the ground after \[67\] seconds. What was its initial velocity when it was fired?
skill: Solve word problems related to freely falling bodies
feedback:
The motion of free-falling bodies is identical on the way up and on the way down. This means that the bullet took \[\frac{67}{2} = 33.5\] seconds to reach its peak and then same amount of time to come back down.
The vertical velocity of a free-falling object at the peak of its height is always \[0\]. So, we know that the velocity of the bullet after \[33.5\] seconds is \[0\] ms\[^{-1}\]. Let us use this information to solve the question.

Time taken: \[t = 33.5\] seconds
Final velocity: \[v_f = 0\] ms\[^{-1}\]
Acceleration: \[g = -10\] ms\[^{-2}\]
The acceleration is negative because the bullet is travelling upwards, for the motion that we are calculating, and gravity is acting downward.
We want to find the initial velocity, which is \[v_i = ?\].

We can use the first equation of motion to make these calculations.
\begin{align} v_f &= v_i + gt \\ 0 &= v_i + (-10)(33.5) \\ 0 &= v_i - 335 \\ v_i &= 335 \mbox{ ms}^{-1} \end{align}

Remember: bullets fired into the air always come back and can be extremely dangerous!

subject: Physics
q: A ball is thrown upwards with a velocity of \[25\] ms\[^{-1}\]. What will be its velocity right before it hits the ground when it is coming down?
skill: Solve word problems related to freely falling bodies
feedback:
This question can be answered without making any calculations. As the acceleration due to gravity stays constant, the ball will have identical motion on the way back as it does on the way up.

The ball starts at \[25\] ms\[^{-1}\] when it is thrown up from right above the ground. As it goes up, its velocity decreases as the acceleration due to gravity pulls it downwards. When the ball reaches the peak, its velocity becomes \[0\] ms\[^{-1}\]. Then, the ball begins moving downwards, starting from a velocity of \[0\] ms\[^{-1}\]. Its velocity increases as it falls down, and once it reaches the ground, its velocity will again be \[25\] ms\[^{-1}\].

subject: Physics
q: A ball rolls off the roof of a house as shown in the picture. What will be its velocity just before it hits the ground?
skill: Solve word problems related to freely falling bodies
feedback:
To solve this question, let us first have a look at all the given information.

As we are dealing with only vertical motion, the ball has \[0\] initial velocity in the vertical direction as it rolls off the roof.
Initial velocity: \[v_i = 0\] ms\[^{-1}\]
Distance/height: \[h = 10\] m
Acceleration due to gravity: \[g = 10\] ms\[^{-2}\]
We want to calculate the final velocity, \[v_f\] ms\[^{-1}\].

We can use the third equation of motion to calculate the velocity. We have to use \[g\] as acceleration and height (\[h\]) as the distance.
\begin{align} 2gh &= v_f^2 - v_i^2 \\ 2(10)(10) &= v_f^2 - (0)^2 \\ 200 &= v_f^2 \\ v_f &= \sqrt{200} \\ &= 14.14 \mbox{ ms}^{-1} \\ \\ \end{align}

subject: Physics
q: When an object is dropped from the top of a building, its velocity _____ as it falls.
skill: Solve word problems related to freely falling bodies
feedback: When an object is dropped from any height, its velocity keeps increasing as it falls. This is due to gravity. Gravity gives all objects an acceleration as they fall towards the Earth. This acceleration is called gravitational acceleration or acceleration due to gravity. It has a value of approximately \[10\] ms\[^{-2}\].

subject: Physics
q: How will the batsman's striking force change the baseball?
skill: Describe the concept of force with its SI units and differentiate among different kinds of forces
feedback: When we apply a force on an object, the direction in which the object starts moving will be the same as the direction of the force. But the force will not affect the weight, shape or colour of an object.

subject: Physics
q:
A potter is creating a vase as shown in the picture below.


He is using force to change the __________ of the clay.
skill: Describe the concept of force with its SI units and differentiate among different kinds of forces
feedback: A force acting on an object may cause the object to change shape, to start moving, to stop moving, to accelerate or decelerate.

subject: Physics
q: When a force is applied on a rubber band _____.
skill: Describe the concept of force with its SI units and differentiate among different kinds of forces
feedback: Force acting on an object causes the object to change its shape or size, to start moving or to stop moving. In the case of a rubber band, a force can cause it to stretch which means its shape changes.

subject: Physics
q: A force acting on an object may not change its _____.
skill: Describe the concept of force with its SI units and differentiate among different kinds of forces
feedback: A force acting on an object can cause the object to change its shape or size, to start or stop moving, and to accelerate or decelerate. The mass of the object is not changed.

subject: Physics
q: The image shows a person pushing and pulling on a door. The exertion of push or pull on an object is termed as _____.
skill: Describe the concept of force with its SI units and differentiate among different kinds of forces
feedback: Force is a push or a pull of an object that causes the object to speed up, slow down, move or stay in one place. All forces are either push or pull.

subject: Physics
q: The SI unit of force is _____.
skill: Describe the concept of force with its SI units and differentiate among different kinds of forces
feedback: Newton is the SI unit of force. It is defined as "the force that will give a mass of 1 kilogram, an acceleration of 1 meter per second squared, equals 1 Newton".

subject: Physics
q: When a horse pulls a cart, the action is on the
skill: Describe the concept of force with its SI units and differentiate among different kinds of forces
feedback: When a horse pulls a cart, its feet act on the earth, pushing its body forward which pulls the cart forward. So, the action is done by the horse on the earth and cart.

subject: Physics
q: You have to push a cart, that is at rest, to a speed of \[15\] ms\[^{-1}\] to win a bet with your friend. But you only have \[30\] seconds to do it. If the mass of the cart is \[20\] kg, what is the minimum amount of force you need to apply on the cart to win the bet?
skill: Define momentum, explain the relationship between force and momentum and solve word problems
feedback:
Let us look at the information we have been given:
\[m_{cart} = 20\] kg
\[v_i = 0\] ms\[^{-1}\], as the cart is at rest.
\[v_f = 15\] ms\[^{-1}\]
\[t= 30\] s
\[F =?\]

To calculate the force, we can use the definition of force as the rate of change of momentum as we can calculate the initial and final momentums with the given information. This definition gives the following formula:

\begin{align} F &= \frac{P_f - P_i}{t} \\ \\ &= \frac{(mv_f) - (mv_i)}{t} \\ \\ &= \frac{(20)(15) - (20)(0)}{30} \\ \\ &= \frac{300}{30} &= 10 \mbox{ N} \end{align}

subject: Physics
q: What is the momentum of the given car?
skill: Define momentum, explain the relationship between force and momentum and solve word problems
feedback:
Momentum is the quantity of motion of a body. It is measured as a product of its mass and velocity. The momentum of the given car can be calculated using the formula for momentum:

mass \[= 1500\] kg
velocity \[=25\] ms\[^{-1}\]

\begin{align} P &= mv \\ &= 1500 \times 25 \\ &= 37{,}500 \mbox{ kgms} ^{-1} \end{align}

subject: Physics
q: A force applied to a body is equal to _____.
options: the rate of change of the body's momentum
the body's mass multiplied by its velocity
skill: Define momentum, explain the relationship between force and momentum and solve word problems
feedback:
A force applied to a body is equal to the rate of change of the body's momentum. If a body has a momentum of \[P_i\] and then an applied force changes its momentum to \[P_f\] in time \[t\] seconds, the applied force \[F\] can be calculated using the following formula:

\[F = \frac{P_f - P_i}{t}\]

\[P_f - P_i\] is the change in momentum from the initial momentum (\[P_i\]) to the final momentum (\[P_f\]).
\[\frac{ P_f - P_i }{t}\] is the rate of change of momentum.

subject: Physics
q: What is the SI unit of momentum?
skill: Define momentum, explain the relationship between force and momentum and solve word problems
feedback:
There are two ways to recall the SI unit of any quantity.

The first is that you memorise it. The SI unit of momentum is: kgms^{-1}.

The second is that if you cannot remember the SI unit, you write down the formula and calculate the SI unit. The formula for momentum (\[P\]) is: \[P = mv\]. Now we simply need to input the SI units of the variables in the formula:
The SI unit of mass (\[m\]) is: kg. The SI unit of velocity (\[v\]) is: ms^{-1}. Now we can calculate the SI unit of momentum:

\begin{align} p &= mv \\ &= \mbox{kgms}^{-1} \end{align}

subject: Physics
q: The momentum of a body is the quantity of motion it possesses due to its _____.
skill: Define momentum, explain the relationship between force and momentum and solve word problems
feedback:
The momentum of a body is the quantity of motion it possesses. The quantity of motion is measured by its velocity and the size of its mass. For instance, a very fast-moving bullet has the same momentum as a truck moving very slowly. This is because, although the velocity of the bullet is very high, its mass is very small whereas the mass of the truck is very big while its velocity is low.
The formula for momentum (\[P\]) is as follows:

\[P = mv\]

subject: Physics
q: Which one of the following is the unit of momentum?
skill: Define momentum, explain the relationship between force and momentum and solve word problems
feedback: The momentum of a body is the quantity of motion it possesses due to its mass and velocity. It is a vector quantity. Its SI unit is Kgms\[^{-1}\] or Ns, as N \[=\] Kgms\[^{-2}\].

subject: Physics
q: A bullet of mass \[20\] g is fired from a gun with a muzzle velocity \[100\] ms\[^{-1}\]. Find the recoil of the gun if its mass is \[5\] kg.
skill: Define momentum, explain the relationship between force and momentum and solve word problems
feedback:
Given that :

mass of bullet \[=\] m \[= 20 \] g \[= 0.02\] kg

muzzle velocity \[=\] v \[ = 100\] ms\[^{-1}\]

mass of gun \[ = \] M \[ = 5\] kg

recoil of the gun \[ =\] \[ ?\]

According to the law of conservation of momentum :

M V + mv \[= 0\]

Putting the given values, we get :

\[5 \times \]V + \[ 0.02 \times 100 = 0\]

or \[5\]V + \[ 2 = 0\]

hence, V \[ = \frac {-2}{5} = -0.4 \] ms\[^{-1}\]

So, the recoil of the gun is \[ -0.4\] ms\[^{-1}\]. The negative sign indicates that the gun recoils in the direction opposite to the motion of the bullet.

subject: Physics
q: A body of mass \[5\] kg is moving with a velocity of \[10\] ms\[^{-1}\]. Find the force required to stop it in \[2\] seconds.
skill: Define momentum, explain the relationship between force and momentum and solve word problems
feedback:
Force and the momentum are related by the equation:

F \[ = \frac{P_f - P_i}{t} \]

here '\[P_f\]' is the final momentum and '\[P_i\]' is the initial momentum

Given that:

Mass m \[= 5\] kg, initial velocity v\[_i = 10\] ms\[^{-1}\], final velocity v\[_f = 0\] ms\[^{-1}\], time t \[= 2\] s, force required F \[ = ?\]

Now \[P_i = \] mv\[_i = 5\] kg \[ \times 10\] ms\[^{-1} = 50 \] Ns

and \[P_f = \] mv\[_f = 5\] kg \[ \times 0\] ms\[^{-1} = 0 \] Ns

Hence F \[ = \frac{0 - 50}{2} = -25 \] N

Thus a \[25\] N force is required to stop the body.

subject: Physics
q: When we apply a force on the ground with our feet while walking, the ground applies the same force on our feet, which is the reason we move. Which of the following laws of Newton explains this?
skill: Describe balanced and unbalanced forces to state and explain Newton's laws of motion and inertia
feedback: Newton's third law states that every action has an equal but opposite reaction. This also means that every force applied on a body will have an equal but opposite force as well. An example of this law can be found in how humans are able to walk. When we walk, we apply a force on the ground with our feet. The ground applies the same force on our feet but in the opposite direction. It is because of this reaction force of the ground that we are able to move forward. The same can be said about jumping. When we want to jump, we apply a greater force on the ground with our feet. The ground reacts with the same force in the opposite direction, which makes us jump.

subject: Physics
q: Two equal forces of \[25\] N are applied to a cart as shown in the image below. What magnitude and direction will the resultant or net force have?
skill: Describe balanced and unbalanced forces to state and explain Newton's laws of motion and inertia
feedback: As the two forces are equal and acting in opposite directions to each other, they will cancel out each other. Imagine that you and a friend stand on opposite sides of a cart and push the cart using the same amount of force. Will the cart move? It will not move because the resultant or net force of your push will be \[0\] N.

subject: Physics
q:
\[ w = mg\]

This equation tells us the weight (\[w\]) of an object if we are given its mass (\[m\]). It is based on which of the following laws of Newton?
skill: Describe balanced and unbalanced forces to state and explain Newton's laws of motion and inertia
feedback:
This equation is another way of using Newton's second law of motion. It is used to calculate the weight of an object when we know its mass. \[w\] is the weight which is a force (\[F\]), \[m\] is the mass, \[g\] is the acceleration due to gravity, which is approximately constant on the surface of the Earth.

\begin{align} F &= m \times a \\ w &= m \times g \\ w &= mg \end{align}

subject: Physics
q: Every action has an equal but opposite reaction. This statement represents which of the following laws?
skill: Describe balanced and unbalanced forces to state and explain Newton's laws of motion and inertia
feedback: Newton's third law of motion states that every action has an equal but opposite reaction. We see this law in action all around us. When a car is moving, its tires push the ground backwards when they are rotating. The ground gives a reaction force, which is equal to the force implemented by the wheel on the ground that pushes the wheel forward. This way, the car moves forward. If you punch a wall, why does your knuckle hurt? The damage should be caused to the wall and not to your knuckle because you are applying force on the wall through your punch. The pain that you feel is because of the reaction force exerted by the wall on your knuckle.

subject: Physics
q: When a force is applied on a cart, it accelerates. The acceleration of the cart is directly proportional to _____, and inversely proportional to _____.
skill: Describe balanced and unbalanced forces to state and explain Newton's laws of motion and inertia
feedback: The question refers to Newton's second law of motion by using a cart as an example. Newton's second law states that a net force on an object causes it to accelerate. This acceleration is directly proportional to the net force (the greater the force, the greater the acceleration), and inversely proportional to the mass of the object (the greater the mass, the lower the acceleration).

subject: Physics
q:
The unit of force N is equivalent to which of the following units?

\[1\] N\[= 1\] _____
skill: Describe balanced and unbalanced forces to state and explain Newton's laws of motion and inertia
feedback:
To calculate the units equivalent to the unit of force N, we will use an equation that has force. In this case, we will use the equation derived from Newton's second law of motion:

\[F = ma\]

Next, we will input the units in this equation and then find out what \[1\] N is equal to. The unit for mass (\[m\]) is kg. The unit for acceleration (\[a\]) is ms\[^{-2}\].
\begin{align}

F &= ma \\ 1\mbox{ N} &= 1 (\mbox{ kg})(\mbox{ms}^{-2}) \\ 1\mbox{ N} &= 1 \mbox{ kgms}^{-2} \end{align}

subject: Physics
q:
The following proportionality represents which of the given laws?

\[a \propto \frac{F}{m}\]
skill: Describe balanced and unbalanced forces to state and explain Newton's laws of motion and inertia
feedback:
Newton's second law of motion states that if a force is applied to a body, it moves with an acceleration that is directly proportional to the magnitude of the applied force.

\[a \propto F\]. The greater the applied force, the higher will be the acceleration.

The law also states that acceleration is inversely proportional to the mass of the object: \[ a \propto \frac{1}{m}\].

The greater the mass, the lower will be the acceleration.

These two parts combined form this proportionality: \[a \propto \frac{F}{m}\]

subject: Physics
q: A student tries to lift \[4\] books using the same amount of force as the weight of the books, which acts downwards. What will happen to the books?
options: They will be lowered as they are heavy.
They will fall to the ground.
skill: Describe balanced and unbalanced forces to state and explain Newton's laws of motion and inertia
feedback:
Let us consider the forces that act on the books. The first force is the weight of the books which acts downwards. The second one is the force applied by the student when trying to lift the books; this force acts upwards and is equal to the weight of the books. This means that there are two equal forces acting on the books in opposite directions, therefore, the forces are balanced. There is no net force.



As these two forces cancel each other out, the resultant force will be \[0\] N. This means that the pile of books will stay as it is: it will neither move upward, nor downward.

subject: Physics
q: The given forces act on the box as shown in the image below. What will be the magnitude of the net or resultant force?
skill: Describe balanced and unbalanced forces to state and explain Newton's laws of motion and inertia
feedback:
The resultant force is calculated by taking the algebraic sum of all the forces acting on a body. This simply means that you add all the forces acting in the same direction and subtract the forces acting in the opposite direction. In the given question, we will add the forces acting towards the left (\[\leftarrow\]) and subtract the forces acting towards the right (\[\rightarrow\]).

\begin{align} F_r &= 10 + 20 - 15 - 10 \\ &= 30 - 25 \\ &= 5 \mbox{ N} \end{align}

subject: Physics
q: What will happen to an asteroid travelling in the vacuum of space with a velocity of \[17\] ms\[^{-1}\]? There is no friction in vacuum.
options: The asteroid will speed up to \[170\] ms\[^{-1}\].
The asteroid will continue at \[17\] ms\[^{-1}\].
The asteroid will slow down to \[15\] ms\[^{-1}\].
The asteroid will slow down and stop.
skill: Describe balanced and unbalanced forces to state and explain Newton's laws of motion and inertia
feedback: According to Newton's first law of motion, an object moving with uniform motion in a straight line will continue travelling in that uniform motion unless it is acted upon by a net force. As there is no friction in space, and no other forces act on the asteroid, <b>it will continue moving with the same velocity, unless an external force acts on it.

subject: Physics
q: Newton's first law of motion is also called Newton's law of _____.
skill: Describe balanced and unbalanced forces to state and explain Newton's laws of motion and inertia
feedback: Newton's first law of motion is also called Newton's law of inertia. It is because this law describes inertia. Inertia is a property of a body due to which the body resists any change in its rest or motion. Newton's first law also states that if a body is at rest, or moving with constant velocity, it will stay at rest or continue its uniform motion unless a net force acts on it. This net force is what works against inertia, which is the resistance of a body to change its state of rest or of uniform motion.

subject: Physics
q: Newton's first law states that "a body continues in its state of _____, provided no net force acts on it."
options: rest or of uniform motion in a straight line
skill: Describe balanced and unbalanced forces to state and explain Newton's laws of motion and inertia
feedback:
Newton's first law states that a body continues in its state of rest or of uniform motion in a straight line, provided no net force acts on it. This means that if a body is resting, it will continue to rest unless a net force acts on it. Similarly, a body that is moving in a straight line with a constant velocity will keep moving with that constant velocity unless a net force acts on it.

If we throw a ball with a constant velocity, should it not continue moving with the same velocity according to Newton's first law? The only reason the ball eventually stops moving is because it experiences friction, which is a force. The frictional force first slows the ball down and then stops it completely.

subject: Physics
q: Newton's first law of motion states that a body stays at rest or moves with uniform speed in a straight line only in the absence of _____
skill: Describe balanced and unbalanced forces to state and explain Newton's laws of motion and inertia
feedback: Newtons's first law of motion states that a body stays at rest or moves with uniform speed in a straight line provided no net force acts upon it .

subject: Physics
q: Two bodies of masses \[m_{1}\] and \[m_{2}\] are attached to the ends of an inextensible string passing over a frictionless pulley such that both move vertically. The acceleration of the bodies is
skill: Derive expressions for the tension and acceleration in a string passing over a frictionless pulley
feedback:
Acceleration of the bodies with masses \[m_{1}\] and \[m_{2}\] attached to an inextensible string passing over a frictionless pulley such that both move vertically is given as :

Acceleration \[ = \] a \[ = \frac{m_{1}-m_{2}}{m_{1}+ m_{2}}g\]

subject: Physics
q: A body has a weight of \[20\] N. How much force is required to move it vertically upwards with an acceleration of \[2\] ms\[^{-2} ? \]
skill: Derive expressions for the tension and acceleration in a string passing over a frictionless pulley
feedback:
Weight \[W = 20 \] N

Acceleration \[ a = 2\] ms\[^{-2}\]

Vertically upward force \[F = ?\]

\[F_{net} = F - W\]

\[ma = F - mg\] ( because "\[F_{net} = ma\]" and "\[W = mg\]")

\[F = ma + mg\]

\[F = m ( a + g)\]

We know that mass \[m = \frac{W}{g} = \frac{20}{10} = 2\] kg

Putting the value of m in the force equation above, we get

\[F =2 ( 2+ 10) = 2 \times 12 = 24\] N

subject: Physics
q: Why are cars fitted with crumple zones in case of a collision?
options: The crumple zones crumple and reduce the force of the impact due to the change in momentum.
The crumple zones enable the cars to deflect off of each other.
The crumple zones reduce the number of accidents.
The crumple zones keep the momentum of the cars low.
skill: State the law of conservation of momentum and solve related problems
feedback: Crumple zones are designed to increases the time frame of collision between cars. This reduces the amount of force generated by momentum and due to the conservation of momentum. This makes the collision inelastic and therefore, absorbs some of the momentum.

subject: Physics
q: In the given scenario, what is the value of \[m_1\]?
skill: State the law of conservation of momentum and solve related problems
feedback:
First of all, lets assume that both the balls form an isolated system. Then, we can apply the law of conservation of momentum to the isolated system.

\[m_1 = ?\] kg ;
\[m_2 = 40\] kg;
Initial velocity of body \[1\] \[u_1 = 20\] ms\[^{-1}\];
Final velocity of body \[2\] \[v_1 = 10\] ms\[^{-1}\];

Initial velocity of body \[2\] \[u_2 = 10\] ms\[^{-1}\];
Final velocity of body \[2\] \[v_2 = 20\]

Law of conservation of momentum:
\[m_1 u_1 + m_2 u_2 = m_1 v_1 + m_2 v_2\]

\begin{align} m_1 \times 20 + 40 \times 10 &= m_1 \times 10 + 40 \times 20 \\ 20m_1 + 400 &= 10m_1 + 800 \\ 20m_1 - 10m_1 &= 800 - 400 \\ 10 m_1 &= 400 \\ m_1 &= \frac{400}{10} \\ m_1 &= 40 \mbox{ kg} \\ \end{align}

subject: Physics
q: A moving ball (A) collides with a ball (B) that was at rest. The balls have the same mass, and after the collision, the ball A stops and ball B, that was at rest, starts moving. Which of the following statements is CORRECT?
options: The velocity of ball A will increase after the collision.
The velocity of ball B is equal to the initial velocity of ball A.
Ball A cannot stop or be at rest after the collision.
The velocity of ball B will remain constant throughout.
skill: State the law of conservation of momentum and solve related problems
feedback:
If the mass of both balls is the same, then in order to conserve momentum, ball B will move with the same velocity as ball A before it came to a stop due to the collision.
Let's assume the mass of both balls is \[m\]. Then according to the equation derived from the law of conservation of momentum:

\begin{align} mu_1 + mu_2 &= mv_1 + mv_2 \\ mu_1 + m(0) &= m(0) + mv_2 \\ mu_1 &= mv_2 \\ u_1 &= v_2 \\ \end{align}

Therefore, the final velocity of ball 2, \[v_2\], is equal to the initial velocity of ball 1, \[u_1\], if the mass of both balls is the same.

subject: Physics
q:
Given that:
\[m_1 = 45\] kg;
\[m_2 = 36\] kg;
\[u_2 = 20\] ms\[^{-1}\];
\[v_1 = 20\] ms\[^{-1}\], and \[v_2 = 0\] ms\[^{-1}\].
What is \[u_1\]?
skill: State the law of conservation of momentum and solve related problems
feedback:
First of all, lets assume that both the bodies form an isolated system. Then, we can apply the law of conservation of momentum to the isolated system.

\[m_1 = 45\] kg ;
\[m_2 = 36\kg;
Initial velocity of body \[1\] \[u_1 = ?\] ms\[^{-1}\];
Final velocity of body \[2\] \[v_1 = 20\] ms\[^{-1}\];

Initial velocity of body \[2\] \[u_2 = 20\] ms\[^{-1}\];
Final velocity of body \[2\] \[v_2 = 0\]

Law of conservation of momentum:
\[m_1 u_1 + m_2 u_2 = m_1 v_1 + m_2 v_2\]

\begin{align} 45 \times u_1 + 36 \times 20 &= 45\times 20 + 36 \times 0 \\ 45 u_1 + 720 &= 900 + 0 \\ 25 u_1 &= 180 \\ u_1 &= \frac{180}{45} \\ u_1 &= 4 \mbox{ ms}^{-1} \end{align}

subject: Physics
q:
Given that:
\[m_1 = 25\] kg;
\[m_2 = 10\] kg;
\[u_2 = 20\] ms\[^{-1}\];
\[v_1 = 20\] ms\[^{-1}\], and \[v_2 = 0\] ms\[^{-1}\].
What is \[u_1\]?
skill: State the law of conservation of momentum and solve related problems
feedback:
First of all, lets assume that both the bodies form an isolated system. Then, we can apply the law of conservation of momentum to the isolated system.

\[m_1 = 25\] kg ;
\[m_2 = 10\kg;
Initial velocity of body \[1\] \[u_1 = ?\] ms\[^{-1}\];
Final velocity of body \[2\] \[v_1 = 20\] ms\[^{-1}\];

Initial velocity of body \[2\] \[u_2 = 20\] ms\[^{-1}\];
Final velocity of body \[2\] \[v_2 = 0\]

Law of conservation of momentum:
\[m_1 u_1 + m_2 u_2 = m_1 v_1 + m_2 v_2\]

\begin{align} 25 \times u_1 + 10 \times 20 &= 25\times 20 + 20 \times 0 \\ 25 u_1 + 200 &= 500 + 0 \\ 25 u_1 &= 300 \\ u_1 &= \frac{300}{25} \\ u_1 &= 12 \mbox{ ms}^{-1} \end{align}

subject: Physics
q: What is the value of \[v_2\]?
skill: State the law of conservation of momentum and solve related problems
feedback:
First of all, lets assume that both the bodies form an isolated system. Then, we can apply the law of conservation of momentum to the isolated system.

\[m_1 = 60\] kg ;
\[m_2 = 40\kg;
Initial velocity of body \[1\] \[u_1 = 15\] ms\[^{-1}\];
Final velocity of body \[2\] \[v_1 = 10\] ms\[^{-1}\];

Initial velocity of body \[2\] \[u_2 = 10\] ms\[^{-1}\];
Final velocity of body \[2\] \[v_2 = ?\]

Law of conservation of momentum:
\[m_1 u_1 + m_2 u_2 = m_1 v_1 + m_2 v_2\]

\begin{align} 60 \times 15 + 40 \times 10 &= 60 \times 10 + 40 \times v_2 \\ 900 + 400 &= 600 + 40 v_2 \\ 700 &= 40v_2 \\ v_2 &= \frac{700}{40} \\ v_2 &= 17.5 \mbox{ ms}^{-1} \end{align}

subject: Physics
q:
The white cue ball on a pool table, travelling at a velocity of \[5\] ms\[^{-1}\], collides with the black ball and keeps moving with the same velocity. Which of the following statements is true? Both balls have the same mass.

  1. The final velocity of the black ball is NOT zero.
  2. No momentum is transferred to the black ball.
  3. The black ball is stuck in its place on the table.
skill: State the law of conservation of momentum and solve related problems
feedback:
As the white ball has the same mass and velocity after the collision, its momentum has not changed at all. This means that none of its momentum was transferred to the black ball.
As none of the momentum of the white ball was transferred to the black ball, it means the black ball did not move and its velocity remained zero. The only reason for that to have happened is that the black ball is stuck in its place on the table, otherwise it would have moved after the white ball collided with it.

subject: Physics
q: Given the following diagram, what will be the velocity of the white ball if the velocity of the black ball after collision is \[5\] ms\[^{-1}\]? Assume both balls have the same mass.
skill: State the law of conservation of momentum and solve related problems
feedback:
First of all, lets assume the black and white balls form an isolated system. Then, we can apply the law of conservation of momentum to the isolated system. Let's assume the white ball is body 1 and the black ball is body 2.

\[m_1 = m_2 = m\];
Initial velocity of body \[1\] \[u_1 = 7\] ms\[^{-1}\];
Final velocity of body \[2\] \[v_1 = ?\] ms\[^{-1}\];

Initial velocity of body \[2\] \[u_2 = 2\] ms\[^{-1}\];
Final velocity of body \[2\] \[v_2 = 5\]

Law of conservation of momentum:
\[m_1 u_1 + m_2 u_2 = m_1 v_1 + m_2 v_2\]

\begin{align} m\times 7 + m \times 2 &= m_1 \times v_1 + m \times 5 \\ 9m &= 5m + m v_1 \\ 9m &= m(5+v_1) \mbox{ m cancels out on both sides} \\ 5 + v_1 &= 9 \\ v_1 &= 4 \mbox{ ms}^{-1} \end{align}

subject: Physics

skill: State the law of conservation of momentum and solve related problems
feedback:
First of all, lets assume the black and white balls form an isolated system. Then, we can apply the law of conservation of momentum to the isolated system. Let's assume the white ball is body 1 and the black ball is body 2.

\[m_1 = m_2 = m\];
Initial velocity of body \[1\] \[u_1 = 5\] ms\[^{-1}\];
Final velocity of body \[2\] \[v_1 = 0\] ms\[^{-1}\];

Initial velocity of body \[2\] \[u_2 = 0\] ms\[^{-1}\];
Final velocity of body \[2\] \[v_2 = ?\]

Law of conservation of momentum:
\[m_1 u_1 + m_2 u_2 = m_1 v_1 + m_2 v_2\]

\begin{align} m\times 5 + m \times 0 &= m\times 0 + m \times v_2 \\ 5m &= m \times v_2 \mbox{ m cancels out on both sides} \\ v_2 = 5 \mbox{ ms}^{-1} \end{align}

subject: Physics
q: How does the law of conservation of momentum explain the flight of rockets by burning fuel?
options: The burnt fuel produces gases that flow upwards. This makes the rockets fly upwards due to conservation of momentum.
The burnt fuel produces gas which is lighter than air and this gas helps the rockets to float upwards quickly.
The burnt fuel ignites the engine which turns the pistons which make the rockets fly.
The burnt fuel produces gases that flow downwards. This makes the rockets fly upwards due to conservation of momentum.
skill: State the law of conservation of momentum and solve related problems
feedback:
The burnt fuel produces gases that flow downwards. This makes the rockets fly upwards due to conservation of momentum.

As the gases flow downwards, they create a downward momentum. In order for the momentum to be conserved, an upward momentum is created on the rocket to cancel the downward momentum of the gases created by the burning fuel.

subject: Physics
q: A gun, laying at rest in a person's hand, is fired. The bullet moves forwards at a terrific speed while the gun recoils backwards. Let's assume the gun and bullet form an isolated system. The momentum of the isolated system before the gun is fired is _____, and after the gun is fired is _____.
options: negative and small; positive and large
skill: State the law of conservation of momentum and solve related problems
feedback:
As the gun and bullet were initially at rest, their momentum was \[0\] Ns before the gun was fired. The law of conservation of momentum states that the momentum of an isolated system remains constant. Therefore, the momentum of the bullet-and-gun isolated system will remain to be \[0\] Ns even after the bullet is fired.

As the bullet moves forward at a high speed, it has a positive momentum. But the gun recoils backwards which gives it a negative moment. When you add the positive and negative momentum they will equal \[0\] Ns as the momentum of the isolated system is conserved according to the law of conservation of momentum.

subject: Physics
q: Which of the following frictional forces is generally the highest?
skill: Define friction and differentiate between rolling and sliding friction
feedback:
All the given options are types of friction. Rolling friction is experienced by objects that roll in a circular motion, like wheels. Air resistance is friction caused due to air molecules colliding with an object. Drag is another type of air resistance which is experienced in air as well as in liquids (such as water).

Sliding friction is generally the strongest friction as it involves constant contact of surfaces, which increases the friction between the surfaces. These skidding marks show that the tyres were constantly in contact with the road and the sliding friction was so strong that it rubbed some of the tyres' rubber off which blackened the road.

subject: Physics
q: In which of the following cases do the wheels of a car face the greatest amount of friction?
skill: Define friction and differentiate between rolling and sliding friction
feedback:
Before the invention of the wheel, it was really difficult to move things around. It is because when you try to drag heavy objects across the ground, they experience sliding friction which is a relatively big force. When wheels were invented, this sliding friction was turned into rolling friction which is a lot less than sliding friction. This is why rolling heavy objects is easier than sliding them. Due to this reason, a car skidding across a road undergoes more friction because it experiences sliding friction.

A car rolling down a street experiences rolling friction which is a lot less than sliding friction.

A car jumping over a ramp only experiences air resistance (friction with the air) which is also a lot less than sliding friction.

A car standing still does not experience any friction at all as it has no motion and no moving force on it.

subject: Physics
q: How does the test tube holder, shown below, hold the test tube?
skill: Define friction and differentiate between rolling and sliding friction
feedback: The shown test tube holder holds the test tube with the help of friction. The holder holds the tube from the sides and not from below. The only reason the test tube does not fall to the floor is because of the friction between the holder and the test tube. The same principle applies to how we can hold cups and bottles in our hands. The friction between the bottle and our hand stops the bottle from falling. On the other hand, if the bottle is wet and slippery, the coefficient of friction between our hand and the bottle gets less, and so the bottle slips even if we hold it with the same force as earlier.

subject: Physics
q: A heavy crate is lying on the floor. A worker tries to move the crate by sliding it across the floor but is unable to do so. Which of the following causes this?
skill: Define friction and differentiate between rolling and sliding friction
feedback: The worker is unable to slide the crate across the floor due to the strong frictional force that opposes this movement. The frictional force is strong due to the weight of the crate which is the gravitational force between the crate and the Earth. Therefore, all the options are correct because all of these factors play a role in making the worker unable to drag the heavy crate. To understand why the gravitational force or the weight of the crate matters here, try to imagine that the crate was not heavy. Would the worker have been able to drag the light crate? Yes!

subject: Physics
q: The force that restricts motion of objects when they rub against each other is called _____.
skill: Define friction and differentiate between rolling and sliding friction
feedback: Frictional forces restrict the sliding movements between two surfaces, and they depend on the texture of the surfaces and the force holding them together. Try it with your hands. Hold both hands together, palm against palm, and then try to slide them against each other. It will be difficult to slide them because frictional force is going to restrict the sliding movement. It will get more and more difficult as you press your hands together harder.

subject: Physics
q: How much is the force of friction between a wooden block of mass \[5\] kg and the horizontal marble floor\[?\] The coefficient of friction between wood and the marble is \[0.6\].
skill: Define friction and differentiate between rolling and sliding friction
feedback:
Mass \[ = \] m \[ = 5\] kg

Coefficient of friction \[ = \mu = 0.6 \]

Force of friction \[ = \] F\[_s = ? \]

Coefficient of friction \[ \mu \] is given as :

\[ \mu = \frac {F_s}{R} \] where \[R =\] mg

Hence, F\[_s = \mu \times R = \mu \] mg \[ = 0.6 \times 5 \times 10 = 30 \] N

subject: Physics
q: Friction is desirable in which of the following cases?
options: when moving parts of an engine rub against each other
when trying to run on a slippery floor
when taking a slide in the park
when driving a car at high speed
skill: List the advantages and disadvantages of friction and various methods to reduce friction
feedback: Friction has both advantages and disadvantages. Friction is a disadvantage when smooth and frictionless motion is desired, such as when taking a slide in the park or when driving a car at a high speed. Friction is useful for us when we try to run on a slippery floor. Friction, in this situation, would reduce the slipperiness of the floor, and will help us maintain our balance and speed without slipping and falling.

subject: Physics
q: Which of the following is NOT an advantageous use of friction?
options: using high friction shoes to climb hills
spinning a cricket ball by rotating it using friction
air resistance slowing down a car
skill: List the advantages and disadvantages of friction and various methods to reduce friction
feedback: All of the given options represent friction being used for a desired purpose. We need friction to run. In cricket, spin bowlers need friction to spin the ball. Mountain-climbers use spiked shoes to increase the friction with the ground as they climb it. Air resistance that slows down a car is not a desired use of friction. It makes the car burn more fuel and use more energy to gain speed. This is why cars are made in a streamlined shape to reduce air friction.

subject: Physics
q: Cars use fuel to run but they also require engine oil. The engine oil is different from fuel because it stays in the engine and has to be taken out and replaced after some time, whereas fuel is used up and does not need to be taken out. Why is engine oil used in cars?
skill: List the advantages and disadvantages of friction and various methods to reduce friction
feedback:
Engine oil or motor oil is used in cars to provide lubrication against friction to the moving parts of the engine. It reduces friction in the moving pistons of a car engine. Motor oil is different from petrol. Petrol is burnt in the engine to produce motion, and motor oil, as stated above, is used to lubricate the engine.

An engine and its moving parts:

subject: Physics
q: Which of the following reduces friction?
skill: List the advantages and disadvantages of friction and various methods to reduce friction
feedback: Lubricating moving parts or surfaces reduces friction between those parts or surfaces. Lubrication reduces friction and increases the efficiency of a machine. Without lubrication, machines waste energy in the form of heat and noise. The image given below shows parts of a machine that require lubrication because they move and rub against each other.

subject: Physics
q: Which of the following situations uses friction to occur?
skill: List the advantages and disadvantages of friction and various methods to reduce friction
feedback:
Running on flat ground requires friction. How? Think about it this way, flat ground faces upwards, and not forwards or backwards. So, any reaction force that the ground can give is only upwards, and not forwards or backwards. Then how do we walk or run forward if the only reaction that the ground can give is upwards? It is because of the friction that the ground offers, that we are able to move or run forward. When we walk or run, we push the ground backwards and the ground responds with a frictional force that pushes us forward.

An easy way to see how friction helps us walk is to think about a floor that is completely slippery (slippery means it will have very little friction). What will happen? Will you be able to run on a slippery slope?

subject: Physics
q: How does the test tube holder, shown below, hold the test tube?
skill: List the advantages and disadvantages of friction and various methods to reduce friction
feedback: The shown test tube holder holds the test tube with the help of friction. The holder holds the tube from the sides and not from below. The only reason the test tube does not fall to the floor is because of the friction between the holder and the test tube. The same principle applies to how we can hold cups and bottles in our hands. The friction between the bottle and our hand stops the bottle from falling. On the other hand, if the bottle is wet and slippery, the coefficient of friction between our hand and the bottle gets less and so the bottle slips even if we hold it with the same force as earlier.

subject: Physics
q: Which of the following does NOT use frictional force?
options: rubbing away a mistake with an eraser
skill: List the advantages and disadvantages of friction and various methods to reduce friction
feedback: Cooking fries in a fryer uses heat energy, whereas writing on a paper, striking a match stick, and rubbing away a mistake with an eraser all use frictional forces. If there was no friction between your pencil and the paper, the pencil would just slide on the paper without anything being written down.

subject: Physics
q:
Which of the following are examples of the use of friction?

I.   a child stopping a swing by putting her feet against the ground
II.  milk falling and settling down at the bottom of the glass
III. a bicycle brake pressing against the wheel to slow it down
skill: List the advantages and disadvantages of friction and various methods to reduce friction
feedback:
A child stopping a moving swing by putting her feet against the ground uses the friction between her feet and the ground to slow down and then stop the swing.
The brakes of a bicycle press against its wheel when we clutch the brake on the handles. The rubbing of the brake against the wheel slows it down using friction.

The falling and settling of milk at the bottom of a glass is due to gravity and not friction.

subject: Physics
q: Which of the following materials lowers the friction between sliding metal plates?
skill: List the advantages and disadvantages of friction and various methods to reduce friction
feedback: The friction can be reduced by oiling the sliding metal plates.

subject: Physics
q: A string is used to revolve a ball around a uniform circular path. What will happen to the centripetal force, \[F_c\], if the length of the string is increased? The mass and the speed are kept constant.
options: None of these options are correct.
skill: Describe and calculate centripetal and centrifugal forces for bodies moving in a circle
feedback:
Let's look at the equation for \[F_c\] to see what effect increasing the radius of the movement will have:

\[F_c = \frac{mv^2}{r}\]

As the radius is in the denominator, increasing the radius will decrease \[F_c\].

subject: Physics
q: A body is moving in a circle at a constant speed. Which of the following statements about the body is true?
options: There is force acting at a tangent to the circle.
There is force acting towards the centre of the circle.
There is force acting away from the centre of the circle.
skill: Describe and calculate centripetal and centrifugal forces for bodies moving in a circle
feedback: Objects that are moving in uniform circular motion always have a force acting towards the centre of the circle known as the centripetal force.

subject: Physics
q: As a result of a constant unbalanced force, a body moves with ______.
skill: Describe and calculate centripetal and centrifugal forces for bodies moving in a circle
feedback:
As the unbalanced force is constant, it will produce a constant acceleration, according to Newton's 2nd Law.

However, in uniform circular motion, the centripetal force causes objects to rotate with a uniform speed but not uniform velocity as the direction of the object keeps changing at every instance. For an object to have uniform velocity, the speed and the direction both need to remain constant.

subject: Physics
q: If the contact of a rotating stone attached with a string breaks, which direction will the detached stone will move?
skill: Describe and calculate centripetal and centrifugal forces for bodies moving in a circle
feedback: When the contact of a rotating body towards its centre of rotation is broken, the body moves in linear motion on a tangential path at the point where contact is broken. This can be seen in option A of the given diagram.

subject: Physics
q: An object is moving with a uniform speed in a circle. If the radius of the path is doubled, what will happen to the centripetal force?
options: \[F_c\] will reduce by a factor of \[4\].
skill: Describe and calculate centripetal and centrifugal forces for bodies moving in a circle
feedback:
Let us assume that the initial centripetal force is \[F_{c1}\].

\[F_{c 1} = \frac{mv^2}{r}\]. Now let's double the radius:

\[F_{c2} = \frac{mv^2}{2r} = \frac{1}{2} \frac{mv^2}{r} = \frac{1}{2} F_{c1} \]

subject: Physics
q: A stone is rotating in clock-wise direction. If its contact with the centre breaks, what will be the direction of motion of the stone?
skill: Describe and calculate centripetal and centrifugal forces for bodies moving in a circle
feedback: When the contact of the stone with the force acting towards the centre will be broken, the stone will fly off on a tangential path in a straight line as shown.

subject: Physics
q: The centripetal force (\[F_c\]) of an object can be calculated by which formula?
skill: Describe and calculate centripetal and centrifugal forces for bodies moving in a circle
feedback:
The formula for the centripetal force on an object moving in uniform circular motion is:

\[F_c = \frac{mv^2}{r}\]

subject: Physics
q: Which of the following statements is true?
options: Inertia depends on the size of an object.
Inertia depends on the density of an object.[MNA1]
Inertia depends on the shape of an object.
Inertia depends on the weight of an object.
skill: Define inertia and identify examples from daily life
feedback:
Inertia is the property of matter due to which it resists change in rest, or change in motion if it is already moving. Inertia depends on the mass of an object. The weight of an object also depends on its mass. Therefore, indirectly, inertia depends on the weight of an object.

An object can have a large size but small mass therefore inertia does not depend on size.
An object can have a large density but still have a small mass therefore inertia does not depend on density.

subject: Physics
q: A person standing in a bus while not holding onto any support fell forward when the bus suddenly braked. Which one of the following best describes the reason for his falling?
options: The bus was not supposed to stop suddenly.
skill: Define inertia and identify examples from daily life
feedback: Inertia is the property of matter which resists change in motion or rest. As the bus was moving, the person inside it was also moving with the bus. When the bus suddenly applied the brakes, the bus stopped moving but the inertia of the person resisted this change and forced the person's body to keep moving forward due to which he could not keep his balance and fell forwards.

subject: Physics
q: Heavy objects are _____ to move because of inertia. Light objects are _____ to stop if they are already moving.
skill: Define inertia and identify examples from daily life
feedback: Heavy objects have greater inertia and light objects have smaller inertia. Inertia is the property of matter due to which it resists change in rest, or change in motion if it is already moving. Therefore, heavy objects have greater inertia and are difficult to move. Light objects have smaller inertia and are therefore easier to stop if they are already moving.

subject: Physics
q: Inertia is the property of a body due to which it resists _____.
skill: Define inertia and identify examples from daily life
feedback: Inertia is the property of matter by which it resists change in motion or rest. Galileo observed that heavy object were harder to move, or to stop if they were already moving. Newton named this property inertia.

subject: Physics
q: Which of the following is the unit of momentum?
skill: Define inertia and identify examples from daily life
feedback:
The momentum of a body is the quantity of motion it possesses due to its mass and velocity. It is a vector quantity.

Its SI unit is Kgms\[^{-1}\] or Ns, as N\[=\]Kgms\[^{-2}\].

subject: Physics
q: When a bus takes a short turn, passengers fall in the outward direction because of _____
skill: Define inertia and identify examples from daily life
feedback: When a bus takes a short turn, the passengers fall in the outward direction because of inertia. Inertia is a property of the body that causes it to resist any change in its state of rest or motion.

subject: Physics
q: Inertia depends on _____.
skill: Define inertia and identify examples from daily life
feedback:
The inertia depends on the mass of a body; the greater the mass of a body the greater is its inertia.

The inertia of a body is a property which makes the body resist any change in its state of rest or motion.

subject: Physics
q: A boy jumps out of a moving bus. There is a danger for him to fall
options: in the direction of motion of the bus.
opposite to the direction of motion of the bus.
skill: Define inertia and identify examples from daily life
feedback:
If a boy jumps out of a moving bus, there is a danger for him to fall in the direction of the bus's motion due to inertia.

The inertia of a body is a property which makes the body resist any change in its state of rest or motion.

q: The number of forces that can be added by head to tail rule is _____.

feedback: The head to tail rule can be used to add any number of forces. The vector representing the resultant force gives the magnitude and direction of the resultant force.

feedback:

q: A man is pulling a trolley on a horizontal road with a force of \[200\] N making \[30^{\circ}\] angle with the road. Find the horizontal component (F\[_x\]) and vertical component (F\[_y\]) of its force.

feedback:
Given that:

F \[= 200\] N, \[ \theta = 30^{\circ}\], F\[_x = ?\], F\[_y = ?\]

As F\[_x = \] F \[cos\theta = 200 cos 30^{\circ} = 200 \times 0.866 = 173.2\] N

and F\[_y = \] F \[sin\theta = 200 sin 30^{\circ} = 200 \times 0.5 = 100\] N

So, F\[_x = 173.2\] N and F\[_y = 100\] N

Given that \[F =10\] N and \[ \theta = 30^{\circ}\]

So, \[ F_y = 10 Sin 30^{\circ}\]

As \[ Sin 30^{\circ} = 0.5\]

\[F_y = 10 \times 0.5 = 5\] N

subject: Physics
q:
In a right angled triangle length of base is \[4\] cm and its perpendicular is \[3\] cm. Find:
  1. length of hypotenuse
  2. sin\[\theta\]
  3. cos\[\theta\]
skill: Describe resolution of forces into its perpendicular components and determine their magnitude and direction
feedback:
Given that: Base \[ = 4 \] cm and Perpendicular \[ = 3\] cm

i. Hypotenuse \[ = ?\]

According to Pythagoras theorem : (Hypotenuse)\[^2\] \[ = \] (Base)\[^2\] + (Perpendicular)\[^2\]

(Hypotenuse)\[^2\] \[ = \] (\[4\])\[^2\] + (\[3\])\[^2\]

(Hypotenuse)\[^2\] \[ = 16 + 9 = 25\]

or Hypotenuse \[ = 5\] cm

ii. sin\[\theta = ? \]

sin\[\theta = \frac{\mbox{Perpendicular}}{\mbox{Hypotenuse}} = \frac{3}{5} \]

iii. cos\[\theta = ? \]

cos\[\theta = \frac{\mbox{Base}}{\mbox{Hypotenuse}} = \frac{4}{5} \]

subject: Physics
q: Apply the principle of moments to calculate the weight of the man. The boy's weight is \[300\] N.
skill: Describe moment of force or torque with the help of an equation and relate the turning effect to everyday life
feedback:
The principle of moments states that for a body to be balanced, or in equilibrium, the sum of clockwise moments has to be equal to the sum of anti-clockwise moments. In this case, the balanced body is the plank of wood on which the boy and the man are standing.

The boy's weight is turning the plank clockwise. Let's calculate the clockwise moment that it is generating:
Clockwise moment \[= W_{boy} \times d = 300 \times 8 = 2400\] Nm.

The man's weight is turning the plank anti-clockwise. Let's calculate the anti-clockwise moment:
Anti-clockwise moment \[= W_{man} \times d = W_{man} \times 2 = 2 W_{man}\]

Apply the principle of moments:
\begin{align} \mbox{Clockwise moments} &= \mbox{Anti-clockwise moments} \\ 2400 &= 2W_{man} \\ \\ W_{man} &= \frac{2400}{2} \\ \\ &= 1200 \mbox{ N} \end{align}

subject: Physics
q: Apply the principle of moments to calculate the weight of the boy.
skill: Describe moment of force or torque with the help of an equation and relate the turning effect to everyday life
feedback:
The principle of moments states that for a body to be balanced, or in equilibrium, the sum of clockwise moments has to be equal to the sum of anti-clockwise moments. In this case, the balanced body is the plank of wood on which the box is placed and the boy is standing.

The box's weight is turning the plank anti-clockwise. Let's calculate the anti-clockwise moment that it is generating:
First we have to calculate the weight of the box: \[W_{box} = mg = 50 \times 10 = 500\] N.
Anti-clockwise moment \[= W_{box} \times d = 500 \times 3 = 1500\] Nm.

The boy's weight is turning the plank clockwise. Let's calculate the clockwise moment:
Clockwise moment \[= W_{boy} \times d = W_{boy} \times 6 = 6 W_{boy}\]

Apply the principle of moments:
\begin{align} \mbox{Clockwise moments} &= \mbox{Anti-clockwise moments} \\ 6W_{boy} &= 1500 \\ \\ W_{boy} &= \frac{1500}{6} \\ \\ &= 250 \mbox{ N} \end{align}

subject: Physics
q: Suppose a spanner needs to generate a torque of \[15\] Nm to loosen a nut. What should be the minimum length of the spanner to loosen the nut if a force of \[60\] N is applied?
skill: Describe moment of force or torque with the help of an equation and relate the turning effect to everyday life
feedback:
As Torque \[ = \tau = F \times L \] , where '\[L\] ' is the moment arm or length of the spanner.

To find '\[L\] ' we use : \[L = \frac {\tau }{F} \]

Given that, \[F = 60 \] N and \[ \tau = 15\] Nm

\[L = \frac {15 }{60} = 0.25 \] m

subject: Physics
q: Suppose a spanner needs to generate a torque of \[15\] Nm to loosen a nut. What is the minimum amount of force needed to loosen the nut using a spanner with a length of \[6\] cm?

feedback:

To find '\[F\] ' we use : \[F = \frac {\tau }{L} \]

Given that, \[ \tau = 15 \] Nm and \[ L = 6\] cm \[ = 0.06\] m

Hence, \[F = \frac {15 }{0.06} = 250\] N

subject: Physics
q: A force of \[150\] N can loosen a nut when it is applied to one end of a spanner that is \[10\] cm long. What is the amount of torque that is generated?
skill: Describe moment of force or torque with the help of an equation and relate the turning effect to everyday life
feedback:
Torque (\[\tau\] ) is determined by the product of force (F) and its moment arm (L). Mathematically:

Torque \[ = \tau = F \times L \]

Given that ,

\[F = 150\] N

\[L = 10 \] cm \[ = 0.1\] m

So, \[ = \tau = 150 \times 0.1 = 15 \] Nm

feedback: Screwdrivers, motors, and drills all use rotation, and therefore, torque to carry out their purposes. A hammer does not require rotation and therefore does not use torque, it uses blunt force to carry out its purpose.

subject: Physics
q: A balanced body has a clockwise moment of \[800\] Nm acting on it. What is the anti-clockwise moment acting on it?
skill: State the principle of moments, and solve word problems related to it
feedback:
The principle of moments states that for a body to be balanced, or in equilibrium, the sum of clockwise moments has to be equal to the sum of anti-clockwise moments. Two anti-clockwise moments of \[400\] Nm add up to an anti-clockwise moment of \[800\] Nm. Therefore, two anti-clockwise moments of \[400\] Nm is correct.

The answer cannot be \[-800\] Nm because a \[-800\] Nm anti-clockwise moment simply means \[800\] Nm clockwise moment. If you choose this option, it means the system will have a clockwise moment of \[800 + 800 = 1600\] Nm and no anti-clockwise moment.

Can you tell why \[800\] Kgm is incorrect?

subject: Physics
q:
What does \[G\] stand for in the given equation:

\[F = G\frac{m_1m_2}{d^2}\]
skill: Explain that the gravitational forces are consistent with Newton’s third law
feedback:
The given equation gives the force of gravitational attraction between two masses. \[G\] in this equation stands for the universal constant of gravitation. Its value is:

\[G = 6.673 \times 10^{-11}\] Nm\[^2\]kg\[^{-2}\]

This constant is not just for the gravity of Earth. This will apply all over the universe, on any planet, in any galaxy or solar system.

subject: Physics
q: According to Newton's law of gravitation, every body or particle in the universe attracts every other body or particle with a force which is directly proportional to the product of their masses and inversely proportional to _____.
options: the square of the distance between the centres of the masses
the distance between the centres of the masses
the square of the distance between the masses
skill: Explain that the gravitational forces are consistent with Newton’s third law
feedback:
Newton's law of gravitation states that every body or particle in the universe attracts every other body or particle with a force which is directly proportional to the product of their masses and inversely proportional to the square of the distance between the centres of the masses.

Please note: the distance is taken between the centres of the masses and not the distance between the masses. Think about it. Right now your feet are touching the ground, therefore the distance between you and the Earth is \[0\]. But you are still experiencing the regular force of Earth's gravitation. It is because the distance between you and the centre of the Earth is not \[0\].

subject: Physics
q: At what distance from the Earth will its gravitational force of attraction be greatest or infinite?
skill: Explain that the gravitational forces are consistent with Newton’s third law
feedback:
We know that when the distance between \[2\] bodies is decreased, the gravitational force of attraction increases. The gravitational force that we experience at sea-level is greater than the gravitational force we experience at high altitudes on top of mountains. Therefore, if the distance between the bodies is \[0\], the force of attraction is greatest. In fact, it is infinite.

To confirm the answer, put the value of \[d = 0\] m in the given equation for the force of gravitation and find the value of \[F\], the force at distance \[0\] m.

\[F = G \frac{m_1m_2}{d^2}\]

Remember: if ANYTHING is divided by \[0\], the answer is infinity.

Mathematically this makes sense, but can you tell why this is not possible?
If the distance between the centres of \[2\] bodies is \[0\] m, it means that both these bodies are at the same exact location, which is physically not possible. Try it yourself. Can you put both your hands in the same location? They will always be next to each other, either on top or bottom or to the sides, but never in the same location.

subject: Physics
q: At what distance from the Earth does its gravitational force of attraction vanish or become \[0\] N?
skill: Explain that the gravitational forces are consistent with Newton’s third law
feedback:
The Earth's gravitational force of attraction acts on a body at ALL distances. The important thing to note is that as the distance increases, the force becomes very small. For instance, the gravitational force exerted by the Earth on the other planets in the solar system is so small compared to the gravitational force of the Sun, that those planets are not affected by it at all.

To confirm the answer, put the value of \[F = 0\] N in the given equation for the force of gravitation and calculate the value of \[d\], the distance, at which the force will be \[0\].

\[F = G \frac{m_1m_2}{d^2}\]

Remember: if ANYTHING is divided by \[0\], the answer is infinity.

subject: Physics

skill: Explain that the gravitational forces are consistent with Newton’s third law
feedback: For the earth's gravitational force of attraction to be zero or to end, the body needs to be at infinite distance. But as an object gradually goes up (and away from the surface), the gravitational force gets weaker but never finishes.

subject: Physics
q: What is \[g_h\] when \[h = 1000\] km?
skill: Explain gravitational field, define weight, and describe the decrease in value of 'g' with altitude
feedback:
To calculate the gravity \[g_h\] at height \[h = 1{,}000{,}000\] m, we will use its formula:

\begin{align} g_h &= G \frac{M_e}{(R+h)^2} \\ \\ &= 6.673 \times 10^{-11} \frac{6 \times 10^{24}}{(6.4 \times 10^6 + 1{,}000{,}000)^2} \\ \\ &= 7.311 \mbox{ m/s}^2 \end{align}

subject: Physics
q: The acceleration due to gravity on the surface of the moon is \[1.62\] ms\[^{-2}\]. The radius of the moon is \[1740\] km. Find the mass of the moon.
skill: Calculate the mass of the earth by using the law of gravitation and solve other word problems
feedback:
Acceleration due to gravity \[ g_m = 1.62\] ms\[^{-2}\]

Radius of moon \[ R_m = 1740\] km \[= 1740 \times 1000\] m \[ = 1.74 \times 10^6\] m

Mass of moon \[ M_m = ?\]

We know that \[g_m = \frac{GMm}{R^2_m} \]

Rearranging the above formula we get \[ M_m = \frac{g_m \times R^2_m}{G} = \frac{1.62 \times (1.74 \times 10^6)^2}{6.673 \times 10^{-11} } \]

\[ = \frac{1.62 \times 3 \times 10^{12}}{6.673 \times 10^{-11} } \]

\[ = \frac{4.86 \times 10^{12} \times 10^{11}}{6.673} \]

\[ = 7.35 \times 10^{22}\] kg

subject: Physics
q: A boy applies a force of \[60\] N to push a sofa by a distance of \[5\] metres. A frictional force of \[45\] N opposes the motion of the sofa. If there was no friction, how much work done would it take to move the sofa in the same amount of time?
skill: Define work, state its SI unit, and solve word problems on work done
feedback:
The total work done by the boy is simply the force he applied (\[60\] N) multiplied by the distance moved (\[5\] m). Which comes out to be \[60 \times 5 = 300\] J. This value of \[300\] joules includes the work done against friction as the boy has to overcome that frictional force to be able to move the sofa.

The net force in the direction of the movement of the sofa is:
\[F_{boy} - F_{friction} = 60 - 45 = 15\] N

So, without friction, the boy would only need \[15\] N to move the sofa by \[5\] m in the same time. The work done would be: \[W_ = 15 \times 5 = 75\] J

subject: Physics
q: A boy applies a force of \[60\] N to push a sofa by a distance of \[5\] metres. A frictional force of \[45\] N opposes the motion of the sofa. What is the work done against friction?
skill: Define work, state its SI unit, and solve word problems on work done
feedback:
The total work done by the boy is simply the force he applied (\[60\] N) multiplied by the distance moved (\[5\] m). Which comes out to be \[60 \times 5 = 300\] J. This value of \[300\] joules includes the work done against friction as the boy has to overcome that frictional force to be able to move the sofa.

The work done against friction is simply the frictional force, multiplied by the distance during which the frictional force was acting:

\[W_{against\ friction} = 45 \times 5 = 225\] J

subject: Physics
q: The amount of work done in carrying a bag of mass 10 kg to a height of \[3.6\] m is _____ .
skill: Define work, state its SI unit, and solve word problems on work done
feedback:
We first find the weight of the bag by using "W \[=\] mg".

W \[ =10 \times 10 = 100\] N

To carry the bag upwards, we exert an upward force 'F' equal to weight 'W' of the bag. Thus,

\[F = 100\] N
\[h = 3.6\] m

work done \[= F h = 100 \times 3.6 = 360\] J

subject: Physics
q: If the direction of the motion of force is perpendicular to the direction of the motion of body, then the work done will be
skill: Define work, state its SI unit, and solve word problems on work done
feedback:
When the direction of the motion of force is perpendicular to the direction of the motion of body, that is, the angle between the force and the distance is \[90^\circ\], then the work done will be zero. As W\[=\] F S \[\cos \theta\]

where W\[=\] work done, F\[=\] force, S\[=\] distance and \[ \theta =\] angle between the force and the distance.

\[\cos \theta\] will be zero if \[ \theta\] is \[90^{\circ}\] because \[\cos 90 = 0\]

W\[=\] F S \[\cos 90 = F S \times 0 = 0 \] J

subject: Physics
q: A car of mass \[500\] kg was filled with fuel that had \[4000\] kJ of chemical energy in total. Using only this fuel it was able to climb a hill which has a height of \[1000\] m. What is WRONG in this scenario?
options: Chemical energy is converted into potential energy.
Cars do not climb hills that high. Jeeps do.
It defies the law of conservation of energy.
Chemical energy is converted into kinetic energy.
skill: Explain conversion of energy in a lamp, a power station, and a vehicle in motion and list three basic concepts of E = mc2
feedback:
The car climbs a hill, which means that it gained potential energy. Let's calculate this potential energy:

\begin{align} \mbox{P.E} &= mgh \\ &= 500 \times 10 \times 1000 \\ &= 5{,}000{,}000 = 5000 \mbox{kJ} \end{align}

We can see that the car gains \[5000\] kJ of potential energy. Since it only had fuel worth of \[4000\] kJ of energy, it could not have gained \[5000\] kJ from it as the law of conservation of energy states that energy can neither be created nor destroyed. It can only be converted. We can not convert \[4000\] kJ of chemical energy into \[5000\] kJ of potential energy. This scenario defies the law of conservation of energy.

skill: Explain conversion of energy in a lamp, a power station, and a vehicle in motion and list three basic concepts of E = mc2

feedback:

\begin{align} e &= mc^2 \\ &= 0.1 \times (3 \times 10^8)^2 \\ &= 9 \times 10^{15} \mbox{ J} \end{align}
